title.zh: 卷六十六 公孫劉田王楊蔡陳鄭傳
title.en: Volume 66: Gongsun, Liu, Tian, Wang, Yang, Cai, Chen and Zheng
title.raw: 卷六十六 公孫劉田王楊蔡陳鄭傳 Volume 66: Gongsun, Liu, Tian, Wang, Yang, Cai, Chen and Zheng

sentences.zh: 卷六十六 公孫劉田王楊蔡陳鄭傳第三十六

translations.literal: Volume 66: Biographies of Gongsun, Liu, Tian, Wang, Yang, Cai, Chen, Zheng, the Thirty-Sixth.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 66: Biographies of Gongsun, Liu, Tian, Wang, Yang, Cai, Chen, and Zheng—the thirty-sixth series.

sentences.zh: 公孫賀

translations.literal: Gongsun He.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun He

sentences.zh: 公孫賀字子叔，北地義渠人也。

translations.literal: Gongsun He, courtesy name Zishu, was a man of Yiqu in Beidi.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun He, whose courtesy name was Zishu, came from Yiqu in Beidi commandery.

sentences.zh: 賀祖父昆邪，景帝時為隴西守，以將軍擊吳、楚有功，封平曲侯，著書十餘篇。

translations.literal: He's grandfather Kunxie, in Emperor Jing's time was Guardian of Longxi; as a general he struck Wu and Chu with merit, was enfeoffed Marquis of Pingqu, and wrote more than ten treatises.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Kunxie had served as Guardian of Longxi under Emperor Jing; as a general he campaigned against Wu and Chu with distinction, was enfeoffed as Marquis of Pingqu, and left behind more than ten written works.

sentences.zh: 賀子敬聲，代賀為太僕，父子並居公卿位。

translations.literal: He's son Jingsheng replaced He as Grand Coachman; father and son together held ministerial rank.
translations.idiomatic: His son Jingsheng succeeded him as Grand Coachman, so father and son both stood among the highest ministers of state.

sentences.zh: 敬聲以皇后姊子，驕奢不奉法，征和中擅用北軍錢千九百萬，發覺，下獄。

translations.literal: Jingsheng, as the empress's sister's son, was arrogant and extravagant and did not obey the law; in the Zhenghe era he misappropriated nineteen million cash of the Northern Army's funds; when this was discovered, he was cast into prison.
translations.idiomatic: Jingsheng was the empress's nephew by her sister. He lived in swaggering luxury and flouted the statutes, and during the Zhenghe period he diverted nineteen million cash from the Northern Army's treasury. When the embezzlement came to light, he was thrown into jail.

sentences.zh: 是時，詔捕陽陵硃安世不能得，上求之急，賀自請逐捕安世以贖敬聲罪。

translations.literal: At that time an edict had ordered the arrest of Zhu Anshi of Yangling, who could not be taken; the emperor pressed the search urgently, and He himself requested to hunt down and capture Anshi in order to redeem Jingsheng's crime.
translations.idiomatic: The court had long sought Zhu Anshi of Yangling without success, and the emperor was demanding results. Gongsun He volunteered to run Anshi to earth if that would redeem his son.

sentences.zh: 上許之。

translations.literal: The emperor granted it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 後果得安世。

translations.literal: Later they indeed obtained Anshi.
translations.idiomatic: They did in fact seize Anshi.

sentences.zh: 安世者，京師大俠也，聞賀欲以贖子，笑曰：「丞相禍及宗矣。

translations.literal: Anshi was a great bravo of the capital; hearing that He wished to use this to ransom his son, he laughed and said, "The chancellor's calamity will reach his clan.
translations.idiomatic: Anshi was one of the capital's most feared swordsmen. When he learned Gongsun He meant to buy his son's freedom with Anshi's capture, he laughed and said, "The chancellor has doomed his whole lineage.

sentences.zh: 南山之行不足受我辭，斜谷之木不足為我械。」

translations.literal: The paths of Mount Zhongnan are not enough to hold my deposition; the trees of the Xie Valley are not enough to make my fetters."
translations.idiomatic: Not every trail on Mount Zhongnan could hold the confession I would give; not every tree in Xie Gorge could furnish stocks for me."

sentences.zh: 安世遂從獄中上書，告敬聲與陽石公主私通，及使人巫祭祠詛上，且上甘泉當馳道埋偶人，祝詛有惡言。

translations.literal: Anshi then from prison submitted a memorial accusing Jingsheng of illicit relations with Princess Yangshi, and of having men practice witchcraft, offer sacrifices, and curse the sovereign, and moreover that when the emperor went to Ganquan he would bury puppets on the post road and uttered malign curses in his invocations.
translations.idiomatic: From his cell Anshi memorialized the throne, charging Jingsheng with adultery with Princess Yangshi, with hiring witches to sacrifice and curse the emperor, and with burying straw effigies along the imperial carriage road to Ganquan while uttering venomous imprecations.

sentences.zh: 下有司案驗賀，窮治所犯，遂父子死獄中，家族。

translations.literal: It was handed to the authorities to investigate He; they exhaustively prosecuted his offenses, and in the end father and son died in prison, and the clan was exterminated.
translations.idiomatic: The case was referred to the judicial officials, who pursued every charge against Gongsun He. Father and son perished in prison, and their kin were extirpated to the last.

sentences.zh: 巫蠱之禍起自硃安世，成於江充，遂及公主、皇后、太子，皆敗。

translations.literal: The witchcraft disaster arose from Zhu Anshi, was completed through Jiang Chong, and thus reached the princesses, the empress, and the heir apparent—all were ruined.
translations.idiomatic: The witchcraft terror began with Zhu Anshi and was brought to a head by Jiang Chong; in its wake the princesses, the empress, and the crown prince all fell.

sentences.zh: 語在《江充》、《戾園傳》。

translations.literal: The account is given in the biographies of Jiang Chong and the Liyuan Heir.
translations.idiomatic: The full story appears in the biographies of Jiang Chong and the Liyuan heir.

sentences.zh: 劉屈氂

translations.literal: Liu Qumao
translations.idiomatic: Liu Qumao

sentences.zh: 劉屈氂，武帝庶兄中山靖王子也，不知其始所以進。

translations.literal: Liu Qumao was a son by a concubine of Emperor Wu's elder brother, the Prince Jing of Zhongshan; it is not known by what path he first advanced.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Qumao was a bastard son of Emperor Wu's half-brother, the Prince Jing of Zhongshan; how he first came to court is not recorded.

sentences.zh: 征和二年春，制詔御史：「故丞相賀倚舊故乘高勢而為邪，興美田以利子弟賓客，不顧元元，無益邊谷，貨賂上流，朕忍之久矣。

translations.literal: In the second year of Zhenghe, spring, an edict to the Imperial Secretary read: "The former Chancellor He relied on old ties and high position to do wrong; he seized fine fields to profit sons, younger kin, and retainers; he had no regard for the common people, brought no benefit to the frontier granaries, and floated bribes upstream—I have endured this long.
translations.idiomatic: In the spring of the second Zhenghe year the throne addressed the Imperial Secretary: "The late Chancellor Gongsun He traded on long acquaintance and lofty rank to act corruptly, seizing prime farmland for the benefit of his sons, clients, and guests. He showed no care for the populace, did nothing to fill the border granaries, and sent illicit gifts up the chain of command. I have swallowed these offenses for years.

sentences.zh: 終不自革，乃以邊為援，使內郡自省作車，又令耕者自轉，以困農煩擾畜者，重馬傷枆，武備衰減；

translations.literal: In the end he did not reform himself; then he used the frontier as a pretext, making the inner commanderies each build carts at their own expense, and ordering cultivators to haul their own grain, thereby harassing farmers, troubling those who raise livestock, injuring horses and wearing out draft animals, so that military readiness declined;
translations.idiomatic: He never mended his ways. He used the border as an excuse to force interior commanderies to build wagons at their own cost and conscripted farmers to haul their own grain, vexing tillers and herdsmen alike, ruining horses and oxen, and sapping the army's strength;

sentences.zh: 下吏妄賦，百姓流亡；

translations.literal: inferior officials recklessly levied taxes, and the hundred surnames fled in exile;
translations.idiomatic: petty officers plundered the people with arbitrary levies until commoners scattered as refugees;

sentences.zh: 又詐為詔書，以奸傳硃安世。

translations.literal: again he forged an edict in order treacherously to deliver Zhu Anshi.
translations.idiomatic: he even forged an imperial rescript to entrap Zhu Anshi by false pretenses.

sentences.zh: 獄已正於理。

translations.literal: The prison case has been judged according to principle.
translations.idiomatic: His guilt has been fixed by law.

sentences.zh: 其以涿郡太守屈氂為左丞相，分丞相長史為兩府，以待天下遠方之選。

translations.literal: Let the Guardian of Zhuo commandery, Qu Mao, be made Left Chancellor; divide the chief clerk of the chancellor's office into two bureaus, to await selection from all under Heaven in distant regions.
translations.idiomatic: Therefore Qu Mao, Guardian of Zhuo, is appointed Left Chancellor; the chief clerk's office is split into two establishments so that talent may be drawn from every quarter of the realm.

sentences.zh: 夫親親任賢，周、唐之道也。

translations.literal: To love kin and employ the worthy is the way of Zhou and Tang.
translations.idiomatic: Cherishing kinsmen and elevating the worthy—that was the way of the Zhou kings and of Tang of Shang.

sentences.zh: 以澎戶二千二百封左丞相為澎侯。」

translations.literal: With two thousand two hundred households of Peng, enfeoff the Left Chancellor as Marquis of Peng."
translations.idiomatic: Invest the Left Chancellor with two thousand two hundred households and enfeoff him as Marquis of Peng."

sentences.zh: 其秋，戾太子為江充所譖，殺充，發兵入丞相府，屈氂挺身逃，亡其印綬。

translations.literal: That autumn the Liyang Heir-Apparent, slandered by Jiang Chong, killed Chong and raised troops into the chancellor's residence; Qu Mao slipped away in flight and lost his seals and ribbons.
translations.idiomatic: That autumn the crown prince Liu Ju, framed by Jiang Chong, executed Chong and marched on the chancellor's yamen. Qu Mao fled headlong and left his official seals behind.

sentences.zh: 是時，上避暑在甘泉宮，丞相長史乘疾置以聞。

translations.literal: At that time the emperor was summering at Ganquan Palace; the chief clerk of the chancellor rode the express relay to report.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was then at his Ganquan summer palace; the chancellor's chief clerk took the post relays to bring word.

sentences.zh: 上問：「丞相何為？」

translations.literal: The emperor asked, "What is the chancellor doing?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked what the chancellor was doing.

sentences.zh: 對曰：「丞相秘之，未敢發兵。」

translations.literal: He replied, "The chancellor has kept it secret and dared not raise troops."
translations.idiomatic: The man answered that the chancellor had concealed the matter and had not yet dared to mobilize troops.

sentences.zh: 上怒曰：「事籍籍如此，何謂秘也？

translations.literal: The emperor angrily said, "The affair is notorious everywhere—what do you mean by secret?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor flared: "The whole capital is buzzing—what secrecy is there?

sentences.zh: 丞相無周公之風矣。

translations.literal: The chancellor no longer has the manner of the Duke of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor is no Duke of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 周公不誅管、蔡乎？」

translations.literal: Did the Duke of Zhou not execute Guan and Cai?"
translations.idiomatic: Did not the Duke of Zhou put Guan and Cai to the sword?"

sentences.zh: 乃賜丞相璽書曰：「捕斬反者，自有賞罰。

translations.literal: Then he bestowed on the chancellor an edict written on jade: "Those who capture and behead rebels shall receive rewards and punishments as prescribed.
translations.idiomatic: He then sent the chancellor a rescript on the jade tablet: "Whoever captures or slays the rebels will be rewarded or punished by regulation.

sentences.zh: 以牛車為櫓，毋接短兵，多殺傷士眾。

translations.literal: Use ox-carts as shields; do not join battle at short range, lest you kill and wound many soldiers and crowds.
translations.idiomatic: Fight from behind ox-drawn barricades; do not close to hand strokes, or you will slaughter your own men by the thousands.

sentences.zh: 堅閉城門，毋令反者得出。」

translations.literal: Hold the city gates fast; do not let the rebels out."
translations.idiomatic: Seal the gates; let no rebel escape."

sentences.zh: 太子既誅充發兵，宣言帝在甘泉病困，疑有變，奸臣欲作亂。

translations.literal: After the heir had killed Chong and raised troops, he declared that the emperor lay ill and imperiled at Ganquan, that he suspected a coup, and that crafty ministers meant to rebel.
translations.idiomatic: Once the crown prince had executed Jiang Chong and called out the guard, he proclaimed that the emperor was gravely ill at Ganquan, that turncoats were plotting a coup, and that the dynasty was in danger.

sentences.zh: 上於是從甘泉來，幸城西建章宮，詔發三輔近縣兵，部中二千石以下，丞相兼將。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor came from Ganquan, favored Jianzhang Palace west of the city, and ordered the troops of the near counties of the Three Assistants to be raised; those within the commandery ranked at two thousand piculs and below, with the chancellor commanding jointly.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor rode down from Ganquan and took up quarters in Jianzhang Palace west of Chang'an, ordering the Three Assistants to mobilize neighboring counties. Every official below two thousand piculs fell under the chancellor's joint command.

sentences.zh: 太子亦遣使者撟制赦長安中都官囚徒，發武庫兵，命少傅石德及賓客張光等分將，使長安囚如侯持節發長水及宣曲胡騎，皆以裝會。

translations.literal: The heir also sent agents to counterfeit an edict pardoning convicts held by the capital offices, issued arms from the arsenal, appointed Junior Tutor Shi De and retainers such as Zhang Guang as separate commanders, and sent the Chang'an convict Ruhou with staff of authority to call out the Hu cavalry of Changshui and Xuqu—all to assemble in full kit.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince likewise forged a general amnesty for prisoners in the capital jails, opened the imperial armory, named his junior tutor Shi De and clients such as Zhang Guang as field commanders, and dispatched the convict Ruhou with a tallies to mobilize the Xiongnu auxiliaries of Changshui and Xuqu, ordering every detachment to muster under arms.

sentences.zh: 侍郎莽通使長安，因追捕如侯，告胡人曰：「節有詐，勿聽也。」

translations.literal: Gentleman for Court Audience Mang Tong was sent to Chang'an; he thereupon pursued and seized Ruhou, told the Hu troops, "The staff is forged—do not obey it,"
translations.idiomatic: The Gentleman for Court Audience Mang Tong reached Chang'an, ran Ruhou down, and warned the Hu horsemen that the tallies were counterfeit and must be ignored,

sentences.zh: 遂斬如侯，引騎入長安，又發輯濯士，以予大鴻臚商丘城。

translations.literal: then beheaded Ruhou, led the cavalry into Chang'an, and also called out the oarsmen of the imperial barges, assigning them to the Grand Herald Shangqiu Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: then executed Ruhou, rode into the city at the head of his column, impressed the palace boatmen, and placed them under Grand Herald Shangqiu Cheng.

sentences.zh: 初，漢節純赤，以太子持赤節，故更為黃旄加上以相別。

translations.literal: Formerly Han tallies had been pure cinnabar red; because the heir bore a red tally, they were changed to yellow yak-tail streamers added on top to distinguish them.
translations.idiomatic: Han credentials had once been solid vermilion; because the crown prince carried a crimson staff, the court reissued tallies marked with yellow yak plumes so friend and foe could tell them apart.

sentences.zh: 太子召監北軍使者任安發北軍兵，安受節已，閉軍門，不肯應太子。

translations.literal: The heir summoned Ren An, the commissioner of the Northern Army, to raise the Northern Army; An received the staff, then shut the camp gates and would not answer the heir.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince summoned Ren An, overseer of the Northern Army, to turn out its garrison. Ren accepted the tally, then locked the gates and refused the prince.

sentences.zh: 太子引兵去，驅四市人凡數萬眾，至長樂西闕下，逢丞相軍，合戰五日，死者數萬人，血流入溝中。

translations.literal: The heir led his troops away, drove the people of the four markets—several tens of thousands in all—to the west gate of Weiyang Palace, met the chancellor's army, and fought for five days; several tens of thousands died, and blood flowed into the ditches.
translations.idiomatic: The prince withdrew, pressed tens of thousands of market dwellers into service, and marched to the western portal of Weiyang Palace. There he collided with the chancellor's host. For five days the armies slaughtered each other until corpses choked the canals and blood ran in the gutters.

sentences.zh: 丞相附兵浸多，太子軍敗，南奔覆盎城門，得出。

translations.literal: The men attached to the chancellor grew ever more numerous; the heir's army was defeated; he fled south to Fugang Gate and got out.
translations.idiomatic: Reinforcements swelled the government ranks until the crown prince's force broke. He bolted south through Fugang Gate and slipped away.

sentences.zh: 會夜司直田仁部閉城門，坐令太子得出，丞相欲斬仁。

translations.literal: It happened that at night the Chief Constable Tian Ren's unit had closed the city gate; he sat by and let the heir escape, and the chancellor wished to execute Ren.
translations.idiomatic: That night Chief Constable Tian Ren's company had barred the gate, yet he stood aside while the heir fled. The chancellor demanded his head.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫暴勝之謂丞相曰：「司直，吏二千石，當先請，奈何擅斬之？」

translations.literal: Imperial Counselor Bao Shengzhi said to the chancellor, "The Chief Constable is an official of two thousand piculs; one should first petition—how dare you execute him on your own?"
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Counselor Bao Shengzhi protested: "The chief constable holds rank at two thousand piculs. You must seek imperial approval before killing him—what right have you to strike on your own?"

sentences.zh: 丞相釋仁。

translations.literal: The chancellor released Ren.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor spared Tian Ren.

sentences.zh: 上聞而大怒，下吏責問御史大夫曰：「司直縱反者，丞相斬之，法也，大夫何以擅止之？」

translations.literal: When the emperor heard of it he was greatly angered; he cast the matter to the clerks and rebuked the Imperial Counselor: "The Chief Constable let a rebel go free; for the chancellor to behead him is according to law—why did you, sir, presumptuously stop it?"
translations.idiomatic: When word reached the Son of Heaven, his wrath was terrible. He had the counselors examine Bao Shengzhi: "The chief constable abetted a traitor; the chancellor acted within the law when he sought his life—by what authority did you countermand him?"

sentences.zh: 勝之皇恐，自殺。

translations.literal: Shengzhi was terrified and killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Shengzhi, panic-stricken, took his own life.

sentences.zh: 及北軍使者任安，坐受太子節，懷二心，司直田仁縱太子，皆要斬。

translations.literal: As for Ren An, the commissioner of the Northern Army, he was charged with accepting the heir's staff while harboring divided loyalty; Chief Constable Tian Ren was charged with letting the heir go; both were cut in two at the waist.
translations.idiomatic: Ren An of the Northern Army, who had taken the prince's tally while his loyalties wavered, and Tian Ren, who had opened the way for the fugitive, were both sentenced to death by waist-slicing.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「侍郎莽通獲反將如侯，長安男子景通從通獲少傅石德，可謂元功矣。

translations.literal: The emperor said, "Gentleman for Court Audience Mang Tong captured the rebel general Ruhou; the Chang'an commoner Jing Tong followed Tong and captured Junior Tutor Shi De—they may be called men of the first merit.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor declared: "Mang Tong of the Gentlemen for Court Audience seized the rebel commander Ruhou; the commoner Jing Tong of Chang'an aided him in capturing Junior Tutor Shi De. Both deserve the highest reward.

sentences.zh: 大鴻臚商丘成力戰獲反將張光。

translations.literal: Grand Herald Shangqiu Cheng fought hard and captured the rebel general Zhang Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Herald Shangqiu Cheng fought stubbornly and took rebel commander Zhang Guang alive.

sentences.zh: 其封通為重合侯，建為德侯，成為秺侯。」

translations.literal: Therefore enfeoff Tong as Marquis of Chonghe, Jian as Marquis of De, and Cheng as Marquis of Du."
translations.idiomatic: Enfeoff Mang Tong as Marquis of Chonghe, Jing Tong as Marquis of De, and Shangqiu Cheng as Marquis of Du."

sentences.zh: 諸太子賓客，嘗出入宮門，皆坐誅。

translations.literal: All the heir's retainers who had frequented the palace gates were executed for their crime.
translations.idiomatic: Every client of the crown prince who had passed the palace gates was put to death.

sentences.zh: 其隨太子發兵，以反法族。

translations.literal: Those who followed the heir in raising troops were extirpated as rebels under the statutes.
translations.idiomatic: Anyone who had marched under the prince's banner was extirpated as a traitor.

sentences.zh: 吏士劫略者，皆徙敦煌郡。

translations.literal: Soldiers and clerks who had looted were all banished to Dunhuang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Soldiers and officials guilty of plunder were transported to Dunhuang.

sentences.zh: 以太子在外，始置屯兵長安諸城門。

translations.literal: Because the heir was still at large, garrison troops were first stationed at the various gates of Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: With the heir still abroad, the court for the first time posted garrisons on every Chang'an gate.

sentences.zh: 後二十餘日，太子得於湖。

translations.literal: More than twenty days later the heir was taken at Hu.
translations.idiomatic: Some three weeks later the crown prince was run to earth at Hu county.

sentences.zh: 語在《太子傳》。

translations.literal: The account is given in the Heir's Biography.
translations.idiomatic: The particulars are recorded in the crown prince's biography.

sentences.zh: 其明年，貳師將軍李廣利將兵出擊匈奴，丞相為祖道，送至渭橋，與廣利辭決。

translations.literal: The next year General-in-chief Li Guangli led troops out to strike the Xiongnu; the chancellor held the farewell rite for him, escorted him to the Wei Bridge, and took leave of Guangli.
translations.idiomatic: The following year General-in-chief Li Guangli marched against the Xiongnu. The chancellor saw him off with the road sacrifice as far as the Wei Bridge and there clasped hands in farewell.

sentences.zh: 廣利曰：「願君侯早請昌邑王為太子。

translations.literal: Guangli said, "I hope, my lord marquis, that you will soon memorialize to make the King of Changyi heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guangli said, "Pray petition early to install the king of Changyi as heir.

sentences.zh: 如立為帝，君侯長何憂乎？」

translations.literal: If he is established as emperor, what long-term worry will you, my lord marquis, have?"
translations.idiomatic: Once he mounts the throne, you will never want for favor, my lord."

sentences.zh: 屈氂許諾。

translations.literal: Qu Mao promised.
translations.idiomatic: Qu Mao agreed.

sentences.zh: 昌邑王者，貳師將軍女弟李夫人子也。

translations.literal: The King of Changyi was the son of Lady Li, younger sister of the General-in-chief.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Changyi was the Lady Li's son—Li Guangli's nephew by his sister.

sentences.zh: 貳師女為屈氂子妻，故共欲立焉。

translations.literal: The General-in-chief's daughter was wife to Qu Mao's son, so they jointly wished to establish him.
translations.idiomatic: Because Li Guangli's daughter was married to Qu Mao's son, the two families schemed together to put the youth on the throne.

sentences.zh: 是時，治巫蠱獄急，內者令郭穰告丞相夫人以丞相數有譴，使巫祠社，祝詛主上，有惡言，及與貳師共禱祠，欲令昌邑王為帝。

translations.literal: At that time the witchcraft cases were being prosecuted urgently; Chief of Palace Attendants Guo Rang reported that the chancellor's wife, because the chancellor had often been reprimanded, had had witches sacrifice at the altars of soil and grain, cursing the sovereign with evil words, and moreover had joined the General-in-chief in prayer to make the King of Changyi emperor.
translations.idiomatic: While the witchcraft inquisitions raged, Chief of Palace Attendants Guo Rang denounced the chancellor's wife for hiring witches to curse the emperor at the sheji altars—she was smarting from her husband's repeated humiliations—and for conspiring with Li Guangli to enthrone the king of Changyi.

sentences.zh: 有司奏請案驗，罪至大逆不道。

translations.literal: The authorities memorialized requesting investigation; the crime reached to great treason and impiety.
translations.idiomatic: The ministers asked for a full inquiry; the charges amounted to capital treason.

sentences.zh: 有詔載屈氂廚車以徇，要斬東市，妻子梟首華陽街。

translations.literal: There was an edict to load Qu Mao on a kitchen cart and parade him, to cut him in two at the waist in the eastern market, and to expose his wife's and children's heads on Huayang Street.
translations.idiomatic: An edict ordered Qu Mao displayed in the executioner's cart, cut in two at the eastern market, and his wife and children decapitated, their heads spiked along Huayang Street.

sentences.zh: 貳師將軍妻子亦收。

translations.literal: The General-in-chief's wife and children were also arrested.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guangli's family was seized as well.

sentences.zh: 貳師聞之，降匈奴，宗族遂滅。

translations.literal: When the General-in-chief heard of it, he surrendered to the Xiongnu, and his clan was then exterminated.
translations.idiomatic: Hearing the news, Li Guangli defected to the Xiongnu, and his entire lineage was wiped out.

sentences.zh: 車千秋

translations.literal: Che Qianqiu.
translations.idiomatic: Che Qianqiu

sentences.zh: 車千秋，本姓田氏，其先齊諸田徙長陵。

translations.literal: Che Qianqiu, originally surnamed Tian, had ancestors among the Tian of Qi who moved to Changling.
translations.idiomatic: Che Qianqiu was born a Tian; his forebears were Qi princes of the Tian house who had resettled at Changling.

sentences.zh: 千秋為高寢郎。

translations.literal: Qianqiu was a gentleman of the chamber for the Lofty Temple.
translations.idiomatic: He served as gentleman-attendant at the Lofty Temple mausoleum.

sentences.zh: 會衛太子為江充所譖敗，久之，千秋上急變訟太子冤，曰：「子弄父兵，罪當答；

translations.literal: It happened that the Wei Heir-Apparent had been slandered by Jiang Chong and ruined; after a long time Qianqiu submitted an urgent memorial pleading the heir's injustice, saying, "A son who borrows his father's weapons deserves the bamboo;
translations.idiomatic: Long after the Wei crown prince had been destroyed by Jiang Chong's slander, Qianqiu filed an emergency memorial insisting on the prince's innocence: "A son who plays with his father's arms deserves a flogging;

sentences.zh: 天子之子過誤殺人，當何罷哉！

translations.literal: for the Son of Heaven's son who kills someone by mistake, what sentence of dismissal applies!"
translations.idiomatic: but when the emperor's own child takes a life through mischance, what grave penalty could possibly apply?"

sentences.zh: 臣嘗夢見一白頭翁教臣言。」

translations.literal: I once dreamed a white-haired old man who taught me these words."
translations.idiomatic: A white-haired elder spoke these words to me in a dream."

sentences.zh: 是時，上頗知太子惶恐無他意，乃大感寤，召見千秋。

translations.literal: At that time the emperor had largely realized that the heir had been frightened and had no other intent, and was greatly moved; he summoned Qianqiu for audience.
translations.idiomatic: By then the emperor understood that the crown prince had acted only from terror, not treason. Deeply shaken, he summoned Qianqiu.

sentences.zh: 至前，千秋長八尺餘，體貌甚麗，武帝見而說之，謂曰：「父子之間，人所難言也，公獨明其不然。

translations.literal: When he came before the throne, Qianqiu was more than eight feet tall, of very handsome bearing; Emperor Wu, pleased at sight of him, said, "Between father and son what men find hard to speak of, you alone have made plain that it was not so.
translations.idiomatic: Qianqiu stood over eight feet, splendidly proportioned. Emperor Wu took to him at once. "The bond between father and son is what other men dare not parse," he said; "you alone have shown it was never what rumour claimed.

sentences.zh: 此高廟神靈使公教我，公當遂為吾輔佐。」

translations.literal: It is the spirits of the Lofty Temple that made you instruct me; you shall forthwith become my helper."
translations.idiomatic: The spirit of Gaozu sent you to enlighten me; from this day you shall stand at my side."

sentences.zh: 立拜千秋為大鴻臚。

translations.literal: He immediately appointed Qianqiu Grand Herald.
translations.idiomatic: On the spot he named Qianqiu Grand Herald.

sentences.zh: 數月，遂代劉屈氂為丞相，封富民侯。

translations.literal: Within several months he then replaced Liu Qumao as chancellor and was enfeoffed Marquis of Fumin.
translations.idiomatic: A few months later he succeeded Liu Qumao as chancellor and received the marquisate of Fumin.

sentences.zh: 千秋無他材能術學，又無伐閱功勞，特以一言寤意，旬月取宰相封侯，世未嘗有也。

translations.literal: Qianqiu had no other talents, learning, or arts, nor battle laurels or accumulated merit; solely because one utterance awakened the ruler's mind, in the space of a month he seized the chancellorship and a marquisate—such a thing had never occurred in the world.
translations.idiomatic: He possessed neither scholarship nor administrative genius, nor any battlefield glory—yet a single sentence that pierced the emperor's remorse won him the seals of chancellor and a feudal fief within weeks, a rise without parallel in history.

sentences.zh: 反漢使者至匈奴，單于問曰：「聞漢新拜丞相，何用得之？」

translations.literal: When a Han envoy reached the Xiongnu, the Chanyu asked, "I hear Han has newly appointed a chancellor—by what merit did he obtain it?"
translations.idiomatic: When the next Han envoy reached the steppe, the Chanyu asked how the new chancellor had earned his post.

sentences.zh: 使者曰：「以上書言事故。」

translations.literal: The envoy said, "Because he submitted a memorial speaking of an affair."
translations.idiomatic: The envoy answered that he had risen by memorializing on a single incident.

sentences.zh: 單于曰：「苟如是，漢置丞相，非用賢也，妄一男子上書即得之矣。」

translations.literal: The Chanyu said, "If it is thus, then when Han sets up a chancellor it is not by employing the worthy—any random fellow who sends up a writing obtains it."
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu snorted: "If that is all it takes, then the Son of Heaven does not choose ministers for virtue—any scribbler who mails a petition may become chancellor."

sentences.zh: 使者還，道單于語。

translations.literal: When the envoy returned, he reported the Chanyu's words.
translations.idiomatic: On his return the envoy repeated the Chanyu's gibe.

sentences.zh: 武帝以為辱命，欲下之吏。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu thought it a disgrace to the mission and wished to cast him to the clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu considered it a humiliation to the court and meant to hand the man to the jailers.

sentences.zh: 良久，乃貰之。

translations.literal: After a long while he pardoned him.
translations.idiomatic: After long reflection he relented and spared him.

sentences.zh: 然千秋為人敦厚有智，居位自稱，逾於前後數公。

translations.literal: Yet Qianqiu as a man was honest, thick, and wise; in holding office he acquitted himself, surpassing the several lords who came before and after.
translations.idiomatic: For all that, Qianqiu was stolid, warm-hearted, and shrewd; in office he conducted himself more creditably than many who had worn the seal before or after.

sentences.zh: 初，千秋始視事，見上連年治太子獄，誅罰尤多，群下恐懼，思欲寬廣上意，尉安眾庶。

translations.literal: At first, when Qianqiu first assumed duties, he saw that the emperor year after year prosecuted the heir's cases, executions and punishments being especially many, so that those below were fearful and wished to broaden the emperor's mind and comfort the multitude.
translations.idiomatic: When he first entered the chancellery he saw how relentlessly the emperor still pursued everyone tied to the crown prince, how the heads piled up, and how terror gripped the bureaucracy. He resolved to soften the sovereign and reassure the people.

sentences.zh: 乃與御史、中二千石共上壽頌德美，勸上施恩惠，緩刑罰，玩聽音樂，養志和神，為天下自虞樂。

translations.literal: Thereupon he together with the Imperial Secretary and those ranked at full two thousand piculs jointly offered birthday congratulations, praising virtue and beauty, urging the emperor to extend favor and grace, relax punishments, indulge in listening to music, nourish his will and harmonize his spirit, and for the sake of all under Heaven take his own ease and pleasure.
translations.idiomatic: He joined the Imperial Secretary and every minister at full two thousand piculs in a court toast, hymning the emperor's virtue and urging him to scatter blessings, lighten sentences, lose himself in music, restore his spirit, and—for the good of the realm—grant himself some joy.

sentences.zh: 上報曰：「朕之不德，自左丞相與貳師陰謀逆亂，巫蠱之禍流及士大夫。

translations.literal: The emperor replied, "I lack virtue; from the time the Left Chancellor and the General-in-chief secretly plotted rebellion, the witchcraft calamity flowed to the great officers.
translations.idiomatic: The throne answered: "I am short of virtue. Ever since the Left Chancellor and Li Guangli conspired in the dark, the witchcraft terror has swallowed half my ministers.

sentences.zh: 朕日一食者累月，乃何樂之聽？

translations.literal: I have taken but one meal a day for months—what pleasure is there in listening to music?
translations.idiomatic: For months I have eaten a single meal a day—what music could I bear to hear?

sentences.zh: 痛士大夫常在心，既事不咎。

translations.literal: The pain of the great officers is always in my heart; now that the affair is ended I assign no blame.
translations.idiomatic: Their suffering haunts me still, yet the matter is closed and I levy no further blame.

sentences.zh: 雖然，巫蠱始發，詔丞相、御史督二千石求捕，廷尉治，未聞九卿、廷尉有所鞫也。

translations.literal: Even so, when the witchcraft first broke out, I ordered the chancellor and Imperial Secretary to supervise the two-thousand-piculs officials in seeking arrests, with the Commandant of Justice conducting trials—I have not heard that any of the nine ministers or the Commandant of Justice conducted a genuine examination.
translations.idiomatic: Nevertheless, when the witchcraft panic first erupted I told chancellor and censor to supervise arrests among the two-thousand-piculs ranks and let the Commandant of Justice try the cases—yet none of the nine ministers or the commandant ever pressed a real interrogation.

sentences.zh: 曩者，江充先治甘泉宮人，轉至未央椒房，以及敬聲之疇、李禹之屬謀人匈奴，有司無所發，令丞相親掘蘭台蠱驗，所明知也。

translations.literal: Formerly Jiang Chong first prosecuted the Ganquan Palace people, then shifted to Weiyang and the pepper chambers, reaching to the likes of Jingsheng and the faction of Li Yu plotting entry into the Xiongnu—the authorities uncovered nothing; I ordered the chancellor personally to dig at Orchid Terrace for witchproof—this is what was clearly known.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang Chong began with the maids of Ganquan, then moved on to Weiyang's harem quarters and clients such as Gongsun Jingsheng or Li Yu's clique, who supposedly plotted to flee to the Xiongnu—yet your bureaus found nothing until I made the chancellor himself unearth straw dolls at Orchid Terrace. You knew the truth all along.

sentences.zh: 至今余巫頗脫不止，陰賊侵身，遠近為蠱，朕愧之甚，何壽之有？

translations.literal: Down to the present the remnant witchcraft slips away unchecked; insidious harm invades the body; far and near practice sorcery—I am deeply ashamed—what longevity is there?
translations.idiomatic: Even now leftover sorcery wriggles free; malign arts gnaw at the court from within and without. I am mortified—what toast to long life can I drink?

sentences.zh: 敬不舉君之觴！

translations.literal: Respectfully I do not raise your goblets!
translations.idiomatic: I cannot in good conscience accept your cups!

sentences.zh: 謹謝丞相、二千石各就館。

translations.literal: Let the chancellor and each two-thousand-piculs official return to his lodging.
translations.idiomatic: Let chancellor and ministers each withdraw to his quarters.

sentences.zh: 書曰：『毋偏毋黨，王道蕩蕩。』

translations.literal: The Document says, 'Without partiality, without faction, the kingly way is vast and smooth.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Classic says: 'Unswayed, uncliqued, the royal way runs level and broad.'"

sentences.zh: 毋有復言。」

translations.literal: Do not speak further."
translations.idiomatic: Let there be no more memorials on this."

sentences.zh: 後歲餘，武帝疾，立皇子鉤弋夫人男為太子，拜大將軍霍光、車騎將軍金日磾、御史大夫桑弘羊及丞相千秋，並受遺詔，輔道少主。

translations.literal: More than a year later Emperor Wu fell ill; he established the son of Lady Gouyi as heir apparent and appointed Grand General Huo Guang, General of Chariots and Cavalry Jin Midi, Imperial Counselor Sang Hongyang, and Chancellor Qianqiu, jointly to receive the testamentary edict and guide the young ruler.
translations.idiomatic: A year and more afterward the emperor fell mortally ill. He named the Lady of the Hook Clasp's boy crown prince and named Huo Guang as grand general, Jin Midi as general of chariots and cavalry, Sang Hongyang as imperial counselor, and Che Qianqiu as chancellor, charging them together with the deathbed rescript to steer the boy who would succeed him.

sentences.zh: 武帝崩，昭帝初即位，未任聽政，政事一決大將軍光。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu died and Emperor Zhao had just ascended, he did not yet personally hear cases; all government was decided by Grand General Huo Guang alone.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu died; the young emperor Zhao took the throne but did not yet hold court. Every decision of state passed through Huo Guang alone.

sentences.zh: 千秋居丞相位，謹厚有重德。

translations.literal: Qianqiu held the chancellorship, prudent, honest, and of grave virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Che Qianqiu sat in the chancellery with the same cautious decency that had won the earlier emperors' trust.

sentences.zh: 每公卿朝會，光謂千秋曰：「始與君侯俱受先帝遺詔，今光治內，君侯治外，宜有以教督，使光毋負天下。」

translations.literal: Whenever the high ministers assembled at court, Guang said to Qianqiu, "At the outset you and I, my lord marquis, together received the late emperor's testament; now I govern within and you, my lord marquis, govern without—you ought to instruct and oversee me so that I do not betray all under Heaven."
translations.idiomatic: At each great assembly Huo Guang would remind the chancellor: "We both swore to the late emperor to shepherd the boy. You hold the civil side, I the inner court—counsel me frankly so I do not fail the realm."

sentences.zh: 千秋曰：「唯將軍留意，即天下幸甚。」

translations.literal: Qianqiu said, "If only the general will be mindful, then all under Heaven will be greatly fortunate."
translations.idiomatic: Che Qianqiu answered only, "If the general stays mindful of his charge, the empire is already fortunate."

sentences.zh: 終不肯有所言。

translations.literal: In the end he would never add another word.
translations.idiomatic: He never offered more than that.

sentences.zh: 光以此重之。

translations.literal: Guang on this account respected him the more.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang respected him deeply for his reticence.

sentences.zh: 每有吉祥嘉應，數褒賞丞相。

translations.literal: Whenever there were auspicious omens, he repeatedly praised and rewarded the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever heaven sent favorable portents, Huo Guang heaped fresh honors on the chancellor.

sentences.zh: 訖昭帝世，國家少事，百姓稍益充實。

translations.literal: Through Emperor Zhao's reign the state knew few emergencies, and the people gradually grew richer.
translations.idiomatic: For the rest of Zhao's short reign the realm stayed quiet and the common folk slowly filled their granaries again.

sentences.zh: 始元六年，詔郡國舉賢良文學士，問以民所疾苦，於是鹽鐵之議起焉。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Shiyuan an edict ordered commanderies and kingdoms to recommend worthy men and literary scholars and asked them what pained the people; thereupon the debate on the salt and iron monopolies began.
translations.idiomatic: In Shiyuan 6 the throne ordered every commandery to send up worthy literati and asked what ailed the populace—thus began the famous salt-and-iron debate.

sentences.zh: 千秋為相十二年，薨，謚曰定侯。

translations.literal: Qianqiu was chancellor twelve years, then died; his posthumous title was Marquis Ding.
translations.idiomatic: Che Qianqiu died in office after twelve years as chancellor and received the posthumous name Ding, "the Steadfast."

sentences.zh: 初，千秋年老，上優之，朝見，得乘小車入宮殿中，故因號曰「車丞相」。

translations.literal: At first, because Qianqiu was old, the emperor favored him: when he came to audience he was permitted to ride a small cart into the palace precincts, and therefore he was nicknamed "Cart Chancellor."
translations.idiomatic: In his last years the emperor indulged him: he might drive a light carriage right into the palace yards, and courtiers nicknamed him the Cart Chancellor.

sentences.zh: 子順嗣侯，官至雲中太守，宣帝時以虎牙將軍擊匈奴，坐盜增鹵獲自殺，國除。

translations.literal: His son Shun inherited the marquisate, rose to Guardian of Yunzhong, and under Emperor Xuandi as general Who Bares Tiger's Teeth campaigned against the Xiongnu; for fraudulently inflating heads taken he committed suicide and the fief was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: His heir Shun rose to governor of Yunzhong; under Xuandi he led the Tiger-Fang host against the Xiongnu but padded the body count and killed himself when exposed, and the marquisate was struck off.

sentences.zh: 桑弘羊為御史大夫八年，自以為國家興榷管之利，伐其功，欲為子弟得官，怨望霍光，與上官桀等謀反，遂誅滅。

translations.literal: Sang Hongyang had been Imperial Counselor eight years; he thought he had brought profit to the state through monopolies and controls, boasted of his merit, wished to obtain offices for sons and younger brothers, nursed resentment against Huo Guang, and with Shangguan Jie and others plotted rebellion; he was then executed and exterminated.
translations.idiomatic: Sang Hongyang had held the censorate for eight years, convinced he had enriched the treasury with state monopolies. He bragged of it, angled jobs for his kin, resented Huo Guang, and joined Shangguan Jie's cabal—when the plot collapsed his whole line perished.

sentences.zh: 王訢，濟南人也。

translations.literal: Wang Xin was a man of Jinan.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Xin came from Jinan.

sentences.zh: 以郡縣吏積功，稍遷為被陽令。

translations.literal: By accumulated merit as a commandery and county clerk he was gradually promoted to magistrate of Beiyang.
translations.idiomatic: He had begun as a petty clerk and climbed step by step until the court named him magistrate of Beiyang.

sentences.zh: 武帝末，軍旅數發，郡國盜賊群起，繡衣御史暴勝之使持斧逐捕盜賊，以軍興從事，誅二千石以下。

translations.literal: Near the end of Emperor Wu's reign campaigns multiplied, bandits sprang up through commanderies and kingdoms, and the embroidered-gown Imperial Counselor Bao Shengzhi, bearing an axe of authority to hunt bandits, acted under military emergency statutes and executed officials down to those below two thousand piculs.
translations.idiomatic: Late in Wu's reign armies marched without cease and robber bands overran the countryside. The robed censor Bao Shengzhi rode circuit with executioner's axe, invoking wartime powers to behead any local magnate who stood in his way.

sentences.zh: 勝之過被陽，欲斬訢，訢已解衣伏質，仰言曰：「使君顓殺生之柄，威震郡國，令夏斬一訢，不足以增威，不如時有所寬，以明恩貸，令盡死力。」

translations.literal: When Shengzhi passed Beiyang he wished to execute Xin; Xin had already stripped for the block and, lifting his face, said, "The commissioner monopolizes the power of life and death and his might shakes every commandery; to behead one Xin in the height of summer would not add to your awe—better to show timely leniency, to make clear your gracious pardon, and let us give our utmost in death."
translations.idiomatic: When Bao's column reached Beiyang he meant to strike off Wang Xin's head. Xin bared his neck to the chopping block but pleaded: "You hold every man's life in your hand; lopping off one magistrate at midsummer wins you no terror. Spare me once and word will spread that mercy pays—that buys you fiercer loyalty than a pile of heads."

sentences.zh: 勝之壯其言，貰不誅，因與訢相結厚。

translations.literal: Shengzhi thought his words heroic, spared him execution, and on that account became closely bound to Xin.
translations.idiomatic: Bao admired the speech, stayed the blade, and afterward treated Xin as a sworn friend.

sentences.zh: 勝之使還，薦訢，徵為右輔都尉，守右扶風。

translations.literal: When Shengzhi's mission returned he recommended Xin, who was summoned as Chief Commandant of the Right Assistant and acting Governor of Youfufeng.
translations.idiomatic: On returning to the capital Bao recommended him; the court summoned Xin as chief commandant of the western preserves and acting governor of Youfufeng.

sentences.zh: 上數出幸安定、北地，過扶風，宮館馳道修治，供張辦。

translations.literal: The emperor often went out to favour Anding and Beidi, passing through Fufeng; the lodges and post roads were repaired and the equipage fully provided.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the emperor toured Anding and Beidi his route crossed Youfufeng, and Xin saw that every relay station and lodge stood ready and every curtain and cushion was in place.

sentences.zh: 武帝嘉之，駐車，拜訢為真，視事十餘年。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu praised this, halted his carriage, invested Xin in substantive appointment, and he administered affairs for more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: Wu was so pleased that he stopped the imperial carriage to confirm Xin in full rank; he governed the commandery for more than a decade.

sentences.zh: 昭帝時為御史大夫，代車千秋為丞相，封宜春侯。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Zhao he became Imperial Counselor, succeeded Che Qianqiu as chancellor, and was enfeoffed Marquis of Yichun.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Zhao he rose to imperial counselor, then replaced Che Qianqiu as chancellor with the marquisate of Yichun.

sentences.zh: 明年薨，謚曰敬侯。

translations.literal: The next year he died; his posthumous title was Marquis Jing.
translations.idiomatic: He died the following year and was canonized as Jing, "the Reverent."

sentences.zh: 子譚嗣，以列侯與謀廢昌邑王立宣帝，益封三百戶。

translations.literal: His son Tan succeeded; as a full marquis he joined the plot to depose the King of Changyi and establish Emperor Xuandi, and received an additional enfeoffment of three hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: His son Tan inherited the fief and, still a full marquis, helped depose the king of Changyi and enthrone Xuandi, earning three hundred extra households.

sentences.zh: 薨，子咸嗣。

translations.literal: When he died, his son Xian succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Tan was succeeded by his son Xian.

sentences.zh: 王莽妻即咸女，莽篡位，宜春氏以外戚寵。

translations.literal: Wang Mang's wife was Xian's daughter; when Mang usurped the throne the Yichun family flourished as maternal kin.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang married Xian's daughter; when Mang seized the throne the Yichun house rode the wave as maternal relatives.

sentences.zh: 自訢傳國至玄孫，莽敗，乃絕。

translations.literal: From Xin the fief was transmitted to the great-great-grandson; when Mang fell it was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: The line ran from Wang Xin down to a fourth-generation heir; it ended when Mang fell.

sentences.zh: 楊敞，華陰人也。

translations.literal: Yang Chang was a man of Huayin.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Chang was a native of Huayin.

sentences.zh: 給事大將軍莫府，為軍司馬，霍光愛厚之，稍遷至大司農。

translations.literal: He served in the Grand General's headquarters as a military major; Huo Guang favoured and advanced him, and he was gradually promoted to Grand Minister of Agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: He clerked in Huo Guang's field headquarters as army major, won the regent's confidence, and climbed to grand minister of agriculture.

sentences.zh: 元鳳中，稻田使者燕倉知上官桀等反謀，以告敞。

translations.literal: In the Yuanfeng era the commissioner of paddy fields Yancang learned of Shangguan Jie's rebellious plot and informed Chang.
translations.idiomatic: During Yuanfeng the paddy-field inspector Yancang uncovered Shangguan Jie's conspiracy and took the news to Yang Chang.

sentences.zh: 敞素謹累事，不敢言，乃移病臥。

translations.literal: Chang had always been cautious in successive posts and dared not speak; he then feigned illness and took to his bed.
translations.idiomatic: Yang was timid by habit and dared not act; he pleaded illness and hid under his covers.

sentences.zh: 以告諫大夫杜延年，延年以聞。

translations.literal: He told Remonstrance Grandee Du Yannian, who reported it to the throne.
translations.idiomatic: He confided instead in remonstrant Du Yannian, who carried the matter to the emperor.

sentences.zh: 蒼、延年皆封，敞以九卿不輒言，故不得侯。

translations.literal: Yancang and Yannian were both enfeoffed; Chang, because as one of the nine ministers he had not spoken promptly, therefore did not obtain a marquisate.
translations.idiomatic: Yancang and Yannian both received fiefs; Yang, though a nine minister, had held his tongue too long and won no marquisate.

sentences.zh: 後遷御史大夫，代王訢為丞相，封安平侯。

translations.literal: Later he was promoted to Imperial Counselor, succeeded Wang Xin as chancellor, and was enfeoffed Marquis of Anping.
translations.idiomatic: He was later promoted to imperial counselor, replaced Wang Xin as chancellor, and enfeoffed as marquis of Anping.

sentences.zh: 明年，昭帝崩。

translations.literal: The next year Emperor Zhao died.
translations.idiomatic: The next year young Emperor Zhao died.

sentences.zh: 昌邑王征即位，淫亂，大將軍光與車騎將軍張安世謀欲廢王更立。

translations.literal: The King of Changyi, summoned to the capital, took the throne and behaved wantonly; Grand General Huo Guang and General of Chariots and Cavalry Zhang Anshi plotted to depose the king and enthrone another.
translations.idiomatic: The prince of Changyi, rushed to the capital, proved a debauchee on the throne. Huo Guang and chariot general Zhang Anshi resolved to pull him down and choose a worthier Liu.

sentences.zh: 議既定，使大司農田延年報敞。

translations.literal: Once the plan was settled they sent Grand Minister of Agriculture Tian Yannian to inform Chang.
translations.idiomatic: When the cabal had fixed its course they sent grand minister of agriculture Tian Yannian to sound Yang Chang.

sentences.zh: 敞驚懼，不知所言，汗出洽背，徒唯唯而已。

translations.literal: Chang was terrified, knew not what to say, sweat poured down his back, and he could only nod assent.
translations.idiomatic: Yang broke into a cold sweat and could only mumble yes.

sentences.zh: 延年起至更衣，敞夫人遽從東箱謂敞曰：「此國大事，今大將軍議已定，使九卿來報君侯。

translations.literal: Yannian rose to go to the robing room; Lady Yang suddenly came from the east bay and said to Chang, "This is a great affair of state; now the general's council has decided and they send a nine minister to report to you, my lord marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Tian stepped out to change robes; Lady Yang slipped from the eastern alcove and hissed at her husband, "This is dynastic life or death. The generals have settled on a coup and a minister of state comes to your door.

sentences.zh: 君侯不疾應，與大將軍同心，猶與無決，先事誅矣。」

translations.literal: If you, my lord marquis, do not answer swiftly and show one heart with the general, you still hesitate undecided—and you will be executed before the deed is done."
translations.idiomatic: Hesitate one heartbeat and you die before dawn—say yes now and mean it."

sentences.zh: 延年從更衣還，敞、夫人與延年參語許諾，請奉大將軍教令，遂共廢昌邑王，立宣帝。

translations.literal: When Yannian returned from the robing-room, Chang and his lady conferred with him in whispers, promised to obey the Grand General's orders, and together they deposed the King of Changyi and established Emperor Xuandi.
translations.idiomatic: When Tian returned, husband and wife whispered their assent with him; Yang then obeyed Huo Guang's orders, and the coalition deposed the king of Changyi and raised Liu Bingyi as Xuandi.

sentences.zh: 宣帝即位月餘，敞薨，謚曰敬侯。

translations.literal: A little more than a month after Emperor Xuandi's accession, Chang died; his posthumous title was Marquis Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Within a month of Xuandi's accession Yang Chang died and received the posthumous name Jing.

sentences.zh: 子忠嗣，以敞居位定策安宗廟，益封三千五百戶。

translations.literal: His son Zhong succeeded; because Chang's tenure had fixed the succession and secured the ancestral temples, he received an additional three thousand five hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: His heir Zhong inherited the title and, because his father had sealed the succession, was granted thirty-five hundred extra households.

sentences.zh: 忠弟惲，字子幼，以忠任為郎，補常侍騎，惲母，司馬遷女也。

translations.literal: Zhong's younger brother Yun, courtesy name Ziyou, through Zhong's appointment became a gentleman, was assigned as mounted attendant at court; Yun's mother was Sima Qian's daughter.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong's younger brother Yun, courtesy Ziyou, entered the palace as a gentleman cadet through his brother's rank and served as mounted attendant; his mother was Sima Qian's daughter.

sentences.zh: 惲始讀外祖《太史公記》，頗為《春秋》。

translations.literal: Yun from the first read his maternal grandfather's Records of the Grand Historian and was fairly adept at the Spring and Autumn Annals.
translations.idiomatic: Yun grew up on his grandfather's Shiji and knew the Spring and Autumn Annals well enough to argue its cases.

sentences.zh: 以材能稱。

translations.literal: He was praised for talent and ability.
translations.idiomatic: The court praised his wit and polish.

sentences.zh: 好交英俊諸儒，名顯朝廷，擢為左曹。

translations.literal: He loved to associate with talented scholars of note, became famous at court, and was promoted to Left Assistant.
translations.idiomatic: He cultivated the capital's brightest scholars, made a name at court, and won appointment as left aide.

sentences.zh: 霍氏謀反，惲先聞知，因侍中金安上以聞，召見言狀。

translations.literal: When the Huo clan plotted rebellion, Yun was the first to learn of it; through the Palace Attendant Jin Anshang he reported it and was summoned to describe the situation.
translations.idiomatic: When the Huo family turned traitor Yun heard first; he tipped off the emperor through palace attendant Jin Anshang and was called in to give chapter and verse.

sentences.zh: 霍氏伏誅，惲等五人皆封，惲為平通侯，遷中郎將。

translations.literal: When the Huo clan was executed, Yun and five others were all enfeoffed; Yun became Marquis of Pingtong and was promoted to General of the Gentlemen of the Household.
translations.idiomatic: After the Huo extermination Yun was one of five ennobled; he received Pingtong county as his fief and was promoted general of the gentlemen of the household.

sentences.zh: 郎官故事，令郎出錢市財用，給文書，乃得出，名曰「山郎」。

translations.literal: By custom of the gentlemen cadets each cadet paid out cash to buy supplies and furnish documents before he could go off duty; this was called "mountain gentleman."
translations.idiomatic: Cadets had long paid cash for stationery and relief before they could leave rotation—office slang called them mountain gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 移病盡一日，輒償一沐，或至歲余不得沐。

translations.literal: If one took sick leave for a full day, one forfeited one bath leave; sometimes for more than a year one could not obtain a bath leave.
translations.idiomatic: A full day's sick leave cost you one of your rare bath-and-rest days; some men went a year without a single leave.

sentences.zh: 其豪富郎，日出遊戲，或行錢得善部。

translations.literal: The wealthy cadets played about by day or bribed their way to choice assignments.
translations.idiomatic: Rich cadets lounged in the markets by daylight or bought cushy billets with silver.

sentences.zh: 貨賂流行，傳相放效。

translations.literal: Bribes circulated and were imitated from one to another.
translations.idiomatic: Graft became habit, each man copying the last.

sentences.zh: 惲為中郎將，罷山郎，移長度大司農，以給財用。

translations.literal: As General of the Gentlemen of the Household Yun abolished the "mountain gentleman" system; he shifted the long tally to the Grand Minister of Agriculture to supply funds.
translations.idiomatic: Yun as chief of the gentlemen abolished the pay-to-leave rule, routed supplies through the grand minister of agriculture, and fed the corps from the public purse.

sentences.zh: 其疾病休謁洗沐，皆以法令從事。

translations.literal: Sick leave, rest, petitions, and bath days were all handled according to statute.
translations.idiomatic: Sick leave, rest days, and audiences were henceforth governed by written regulation.

sentences.zh: 郎、謁者有罪過，輒奏免，薦舉其高弟有行能者，至郡守、九卿。

translations.literal: When gentlemen cadets or ushers had offenses they were memorialized for dismissal; he recommended for promotion those of high rank and proven conduct, up to governors and the nine ministers.
translations.idiomatic: He cashiered erring cadets and ushers by memorial and promoted the ablest, some all the way to governors and nine ministers.

sentences.zh: 郎官化之，莫不自厲，絕請謁貨賂之端，令行禁止，宮殿之內翕然同聲。

translations.literal: The cadets were transformed by him; none failed to steel himself, the channel of private requests and bribes was cut off, orders were obeyed and prohibitions enforced, and within the palace there was unanimous accord.
translations.idiomatic: The corps straightened under him: no more backstairs silver, orders ran clean, and the inner palace spoke with one voice.

sentences.zh: 由是擢為諸吏光祿勳，親近用事。

translations.literal: On this account he was promoted Superintendent of the Palace and Privy Treasurer of the Bearer of Gentlemen, became intimate at power's side.
translations.idiomatic: He was therefore raised to superintendent of the palace and privy treasurer of the gentlemen, standing at the emperor's elbow.

sentences.zh: 初，惲受父財五百萬，及身封侯，皆以分宗族。

translations.literal: At first Yun received five million cash from his father; when he himself was enfeoffed marquis he distributed all of it to his kinsmen.
translations.idiomatic: His father had left him five million cash; the day he became a marquis he gave every coin to his clan.

sentences.zh: 後母無子，財亦數百萬，死皆子惲，惲盡復分後母昆弟。

translations.literal: His stepmother had no children; her property also ran to several million; on her death it all passed to Yun, who distributed it again among his stepmother's brothers.
translations.idiomatic: His childless stepmother owned millions more; when she died it fell to Yun, who shared it with her brothers.

sentences.zh: 再受訾千餘萬，皆以分施。

translations.literal: A second time he received assets exceeding ten million, and he distributed all of it in gifts.
translations.idiomatic: Twice more he inherited sums over ten million and gave them away as fast as they arrived.

sentences.zh: 其輕財好義如此。

translations.literal: Thus lightly he treated wealth and loved righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: That was how lightly he held money and how dearly he held honor.

sentences.zh: 惲居殿中，廉潔無私，郎官稱公平。

translations.literal: When Yun was in the palace he was incorrupt and without private favor; the gentlemen cadets praised his fairness.
translations.idiomatic: Inside the palace he took no bribes and showed no favoritism; the cadets called him just.

sentences.zh: 然惲伐其行治，又性刻害，好發人陰伏，同位有忤己者，必欲害之，以其能高人。

translations.literal: Yet Yun boasted of his conduct and governance; his nature was harsh and injurious; he loved to expose others' hidden faults; any colleague who crossed him he was set on harming, using his talent to lord over others.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he swaggered over his own rectitude, savored cutting others down, ferreted out private scandal, and ruined any peer who crossed him—his cleverness became a bludgeon.

sentences.zh: 由是多怨於朝廷，與太僕戴長樂相失，卒以是敗。

translations.literal: Therefore he accumulated many enemies at court; he fell out with Grand Coachman Dai Changle and in the end was ruined on that account.
translations.idiomatic: He made enemies everywhere, quarreled with grand coachman Dai Changle, and in the end that feud destroyed him.

sentences.zh: 長樂者，宣帝在民間時與相知，及即位，拔擢親近。

translations.literal: Changle had known the emperor when he was still among the common people; when Xuandi mounted the throne he pulled him up to stay close.
translations.idiomatic: Dai had been Xuandi's companion in the lanes before the throne; the new emperor raised him to ride at his side.

sentences.zh: 長樂嘗使行事肄宗廟，還謂掾史曰：「我親面見受詔，副帝肄，秺侯御。」

translations.literal: Changle was once sent to act as deputy in the imperial rehearsal at the ancestral temple; returning he said to his clerks, "I personally was received in audience and given edict, assisting the Son of Heaven in the rehearsal while the Marquis of Du held the reins."
translations.idiomatic: Once, after Dai had served as the emperor's deputy in the temple drill, he bragged to his clerks that he had taken edict face to face while the marquis of Du drove the chariot.

sentences.zh: 人有上書告長樂非所宜言，事下廷尉。

translations.literal: Someone memorialized accusing Changle of having spoken beyond what was proper; the matter was sent down to the Commandant of Justice.
translations.idiomatic: A tipster denounced that boast as lèse-majesté; the case went to the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 長樂疑惲教人告之，亦上書告惲罪。

translations.literal: Changle suspected Yun had incited the accusation and likewise memorialized accusing Yun of crimes.
translations.idiomatic: Dai decided Yun had put the accuser up to it and struck back with his own bill of indictment.

sentences.zh: 高昌侯車奔入北掖門，惲語富平侯張延壽曰：「聞前曾有奔車抵殿門，門關折，馬死，而昭帝崩。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Gaochang's chariot galloped into the north vee gate; Yun said to Marquis of Fuping Zhang Yanshou, "I have heard that once before a runaway chariot rammed a palace gate, the gate bolt broke, the horse died, and Emperor Zhao perished."
translations.idiomatic: When the marquis of Gaochang's carriage bolted through the north vee gate, Yun remarked to Zhang Yanshou of Fuping, "They say the last time a team careered into a palace gate, the bar snapped, the horses fell, and Emperor Zhao was dead within days."

sentences.zh: 今復如此，天時，非人力也。」

translations.literal: "Now it is so again—this is heaven's timing, not human effort."
translations.idiomatic: "Here we go again—omens, not accidents."

sentences.zh: 左馮翊韓延壽有罪下獄，惲上書訟延壽。

translations.literal: When Han Yanshou, Governor of the Left [Fengyi], was charged and cast into prison, Yun memorialized in his defence.
translations.idiomatic: When Han Yanshou, governor of left Fengyi, landed in prison, Yun filed a memorial pleading his innocence.

sentences.zh: 郎中丘常謂惲曰：「聞君侯訟韓馮翊，當得活乎？」

translations.literal: Gentleman of the Household Qiu Chang said to Yun, "I hear your lordship is pleading for Governor Han—will he get off alive?"
translations.idiomatic: Cadet Qiu Chang asked whether Yun thought Han would walk out alive.

sentences.zh: 惲曰：「事何容易！

translations.literal: Yun said, "How easy do you think the matter is!"
translations.idiomatic: Yun snapped, "You think that is an easy fix?"

sentences.zh: 脛脛者未必全也。

translations.literal: Even the upright are not necessarily spared.
translations.idiomatic: Even honest men do not always walk free.

sentences.zh: 我不能自保，真人所謂鼠不容穴銜窶數者也。」

translations.literal: "I cannot even protect myself—what men call a rat with no hole left gnawing tattered rags."
translations.idiomatic: "I can barely save my own skin—the proverb about the cornered rat chewing rags fits me too well."

sentences.zh: 又中書謁者令宣持單于使者語，視諸將軍、中朝二千石。

translations.literal: Again the Director of Attendants for the Palace Xuan conveyed the Xiongnu envoys' words and displayed them to the generals and the inner court ministers at two thousand piculs.
translations.idiomatic: Again the palace usher director Xuan relayed the Xiongnu envoys' gossip to the generals and inner-court ministers of two thousand piculs.

sentences.zh: 惲曰：「冒頓單于得漢美食好物，謂之殠惡，單于不來明甚。」

translations.literal: Yun said, "The Modun Chanyu found Han's fine foods and treasures stinking and vile; it is abundantly clear the Chanyu will not come south."
translations.idiomatic: Yun added that Modun had called Han delicacies rotten and foul—proof the Chanyu would never bother to visit.

sentences.zh: 惲上觀西閣上畫人，指桀、紂畫謂樂昌侯王武曰：「天子過此，一二問其過，可以得師矣。」

translations.literal: In the upper gallery Yun studied the paintings on the western pavilion, pointed to the portraits of Jie and Zhou and said to Marquis of Lechang Wang Wu, "When the Son of Heaven passes here, if he asks once or twice about their faults, he may gain his teachers."
translations.idiomatic: Touring the western gallery he pointed at the portraits of Jie and Zhou and told Wang Wu of Lechang, "If our ruler paused here and asked what those two did wrong, he might learn something."

sentences.zh: 畫人有堯、舜、禹、湯，不稱而舉桀、紂。

translations.literal: The hall held portraits of Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang, yet he named not them but Jie and Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: The wall also showed Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang—yet he singled out the tyrants.

sentences.zh: 惲聞匈奴降者道單于見殺，惲曰：「得不肖君，大臣為畫善計不用，自令身無處所。

translations.literal: When Yun heard from Xiongnu who had surrendered that the Chanyu had been killed, he said, "When you get a worthless ruler and his ministers lay good plans yet he will not use them, you end with nowhere to turn.
translations.idiomatic: Hearing from surrendered nomads that their Chanyu had been murdered, Yun said, "Give a realm to a fool who ignores good counsel and even his ministers have nowhere to stand.

sentences.zh: 若秦時但任小臣，誅殺忠良，竟以滅亡；

translations.literal: It was like Qin times, when they trusted only petty officials, slaughtered the loyal and good, and in the end perished;
translations.idiomatic: Qin fell because it trusted petty men and butchered its loyalists;

sentences.zh: 令親任大臣，即至今耳。

translations.literal: had they instead entrusted great ministers, they would still be ruling today.
translations.idiomatic: had it trusted great ministers it might still reign today.

sentences.zh: 古與今如一丘之貉。」

translations.literal: "Ancient and modern are badgers from the same hill."
translations.idiomatic: "Past and present are badgers dug from the same burrow."

sentences.zh: 惲妄引亡國以誹謗當世，無人臣禮。

translations.literal: Yun recklessly cited fallen states to slander the present age and showed no deportment befitting a subject.
translations.idiomatic: He had invoked ruined dynasties to mock his own sovereign—conduct no subject may offer.

sentences.zh: 又語長樂曰：「正月以來，天陰不雨，此《春秋》所記，夏侯君所言。

translations.literal: He also told Changle, "Since the first month the sky has been overcast without rain—what the Spring and Autumn records, what Lord Xiahou spoke of.
translations.idiomatic: He also told Dai, "Since new year the sky has stayed black without rain—the very sign the Spring and Autumn and Lord Xiahou warned against.

sentences.zh: 行必不至河東矣。」

translations.literal: "The journey surely will not reach Hedong."
translations.idiomatic: "The imperial train will never reach Hedong."

sentences.zh: 以主上為戲語，尤悖逆絕理。

translations.literal: To treat the sovereign as the butt of jest was supremely perverse and defied all principle.
translations.idiomatic: Jesting about the Son of Heaven crossed every line of loyalty.

sentences.zh: 事下廷尉。

translations.literal: The case was sent to the Commandant of Justice.
translations.idiomatic: The file went to the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 廷尉定國考問，左驗明白，奏：

translations.literal: The Commandant of Justice, Yu Dinggu, examined him; corroboration gathered on every side was clear, and he memorialized:
translations.idiomatic: Commandant Yu Dinggu questioned him until the corroborating testimony was plain, then reported to the throne:

sentences.zh: 惲不服罪，而召戶將尊，欲令戒飭富平侯延壽，曰：「太僕定有死罪數事，朝暮人也。

translations.literal: Yun refused to confess guilt but summoned the household commandant Zun, wishing to have him warn Marquis of Fuping Yanshou, saying, "The Grand Coachman certainly has several capital crimes; he is a man who counts his days by morning and evening.
translations.idiomatic: Yun refused to plead guilty but called in steward Zun to coach Zhang Yanshou: "The grand coachman sits on multiple capital offenses—he is living on borrowed time.

sentences.zh: 惲幸與富平侯婚姻，今獨三人坐語，侯言『時不聞惲語』，自與太僕相觸也。」

translations.literal: "I am fortunate to be affined to Marquis of Fuping; now the three of us sat talking alone; if the marquis says 'he did not hear Yun's words at the time,' he clashes directly with the Grand Coachman."
translations.idiomatic: I am tied to Fuping by marriage; if the marquis later swears he never heard me speak, he perjures himself against Dai."

sentences.zh: 尊曰：「不可。」

translations.literal: Zun said, "It cannot be done."
translations.idiomatic: Zun refused.

sentences.zh: 惲怒，持大刀，曰：「蒙富平侯力，得族罪！

translations.literal: Yun in anger seized a long knife and said, "Relying on Marquis of Fuping's pull I escaped clan execution!"
translations.idiomatic: Yun drew a long knife and snarled, "Only Fuping's favor kept my clan alive!"

sentences.zh: 毋洩惲語，令太僕聞之亂餘事。」

translations.literal: "Do not leak what Yun said—let the Grand Coachman hear and he will confuse the rest of the case."
translations.idiomatic: "Keep your mouth shut or Dai will wreck the rest of our story."

sentences.zh: 惲幸得列九卿諸吏，宿衛近臣，上所信任，與聞政事，不竭忠愛，盡臣子義，而妄怨望，稱引為訞惡言，大逆不道，請逮捕治。

translations.literal: Though Yun had ranked among the nine ministers and superintendents, a night guard and trusted confidant of the sovereign who heard state affairs, he did not exhaust loyal devotion or fulfill a son-of-the-soil's duty, but groundlessly nursed resentment and spouted malignant slander—it was great treason and impiety; let him be arrested and tried.
translations.idiomatic: Though Yun had stood among the nine ministers and palace superintendents, heard every state secret, and enjoyed imperial trust, he had not repaid that trust with loyalty but spread venomous slander—conduct that amounted to great treason; Yu Dinggu asked leave to clap him in chains.

sentences.zh: 上不忍加誅，有詔皆免惲、長樂為庶人。

translations.literal: The emperor could not bear to execute them; an edict degraded Yun and Changle alike to commoners.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi shrank from blood and issued an edict stripping both Yun and Dai to commoner status.

sentences.zh: 惲既失爵位，家居治產業，起室宅，以財自娛。

translations.literal: Once Yun had lost rank and title, he dwelt at home, managed his estate, raised house and hall, and took his pleasure in wealth.
translations.idiomatic: Stripped of rank, Yun retired to his lands, built fine halls, and amused himself with his fortune.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，其友人安定太守西河孫會宗，知略士也，與惲書諫戒之，為言大臣廢退，當闔門惶懼，為可憐之意，不當治產業，通賓客，有稱譽。

translations.literal: After more than a year his friend Sun Huizong, Guardian of Anding from Xihe, a man of strategy and insight, wrote Yun a letter of remonstrance and warning, saying that when great ministers are dismissed and retired they ought to shut their gates in fear and trembling, showing an attitude fit to win pity, and ought not to manage estates, traffic with guests, or invite praise.
translations.idiomatic: A year later his friend Sun Huizong, governor of Anding, a shrewd man from Xihe, wrote urging him to live like a disgraced minister—bar the gates, look wretched, beg sympathy—and not to rebuild a fortune, throw open his house to clients, or court a reputation.

sentences.zh: 惲宰相子，少顯朝廷，一朝以暗昧語言見廢，內懷不服，報會宗書曰：

translations.literal: Yun was a chancellor's son who had shown himself early at court; in one morning, on account of obscure speech, he was cast aside; inwardly he did not submit, and he replied to Huizong's letter, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Yun was a prime minister's son who had glittered at court while young; one murky phrase had ruined him, and resentment still seethed as he answered Sun Huizong:

sentences.zh: 惲材朽行穢，文質無所底，幸賴先人余業得備宿衛，遭遇時變以獲爵位，終非其任，卒與禍會。

translations.literal: Yun's timber is rotten and his conduct foul; in pattern and substance he has reached no bottom; fortunately relying on his forebears' remaining estate he was able to serve in night guard, met with the changes of the times and thereby obtained rank and title, but in the end it was not his to bear, and he ended meeting calamity.
translations.idiomatic: I am warped wood and foul conduct, neither learned nor presentable; I inherited a name that bought me a slot in the palace guard, then luck and turmoil raised me to nobility—posts I was never fit for, and disaster followed.

sentences.zh: 足下哀其愚，蒙賜書，教督以所不及，殷勤甚厚。

translations.literal: You pity my stupidity, have favoured me with a letter, instructing and overseeing what I lack—your earnest kindness is very great.
translations.idiomatic: You pity my folly and lecture me where I fall short—kindness I do not forget.

sentences.zh: 然竊恨足下不深惟其終始，而猥隨俗之毀譽也。

translations.literal: Yet I privately resent that you have not deeply pondered the beginning and end, but vulgarly follow the world's praise and blame.
translations.idiomatic: Yet I resent that you never traced my story from start to finish but parrot the world's gossip.

sentences.zh: 言鄙陋之愚心，若逆指而文過，默而息乎，恐違孔氏「各言爾志」之義，故敢略陳其愚，唯君子察焉！

translations.literal: If I speak my base and shallow foolish heart, it is as if I oppose your finger and gloss my fault; if I am silent and rest, I fear I violate Confucius's meaning of 'each declare your intent'—therefore I dare briefly state my folly; may the gentleman examine it!
translations.idiomatic: If I hide my thoughts I twist your meaning; if I hold my tongue I break Confucius's rule that each man should speak his mind—so I lay my folly before you; judge it as you will.

sentences.zh: 惲家方隆盛時，乘硃輪者十人，位在列卿，爵為通侯，總領從官，與聞政事，曾不能以此時有所建明，以宣德化，又不能與群僚同心並力，陪輔朝廷之遺忘，已負竊位素餐之責久矣。

translations.literal: When Yun's family was at its height of splendour, those who rode vermillion-wheeled carriages were ten in number; his position stood among the ranked ministers, his noble rank was full marquis, he headed the attendant officials and heard government affairs—yet he could not at that time achieve anything to make clear virtue and transformation, nor could he join his colleagues in one heart and combined strength, assisting the court in what it overlooked—already for long he bore the blame of stealing office and eating salary empty.
translations.idiomatic: When my house flourished, ten kinsmen rode the vermilion-wheeled carriages of the highest nobility; I myself ranked among the nine ministers, bore a full marquisate, commanded the emperor's bodyguard, and sat in on policy—yet I never once spoke a word that might spread the court's virtue, never rallied my peers to patch what the throne forgot. Long before my fall I deserved the charge of drawing pay for an empty chair.

sentences.zh: 懷祿貪勢，不能自退，遭遇變故，橫被口語，身幽北闕，妻子滿獄。

translations.literal: He embraced salary and coveted power, could not retire of himself, met with sudden change, was smeared by loose talk, his person was confined at the northern gate tower, wife and children filled the prisons.
translations.idiomatic: I clung to office and salary, would not step down, then a storm of slander locked me in the north gate prison while my wife and children filled the cells.

sentences.zh: 當此之時，自以夷滅不足以塞責，豈意得全首領，復奉先人之丘墓乎？

translations.literal: At that time he thought even extermination would not suffice to fill his responsibility—how could he have expected to keep his head whole and again tend his forebears' tombs?
translations.idiomatic: I was sure clan execution would not atone for my guilt—who dreamed I would keep my head and walk my father's hill again?

sentences.zh: 伏惟聖主之恩，不可勝量。

translations.literal: Prostrate, he reflected that the sage ruler's grace cannot be measured.
translations.idiomatic: I bow to a grace no man can measure.

sentences.zh: 君子游道，樂以忘憂；

translations.literal: The gentleman who roams the Way finds joy and forgets sorrow.
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman who walks the Way finds joy and forgets his cares.

sentences.zh: 小人全軀，說以忘罪。

translations.literal: The petty man who preserves his body finds pleasure and forgets guilt.
translations.idiomatic: The small man who saves his skin finds comfort and forgets his shame.

sentences.zh: 竊自思念，過已大矣，行已虧矣，長為農夫以沒世矣。

translations.literal: Privately he thought: my fault is already great, my conduct already impaired; I shall long be a farmer to the end of my days.
translations.idiomatic: I told myself my crimes were grave and my name ruined—that I would die a ploughman.

sentences.zh: 是故身率妻子，戮力耕桑，灌園治產，以給公上，不意當復用此為譏議也。

translations.literal: Therefore he personally led wife and children, exerted strength in ploughing and sericulture, irrigated the garden and managed production, in order to supply the public treasury—he did not expect again to be faulted on this account.
translations.idiomatic: So I took my family to the fields, hoed and mulched, watered the garden, and sent the tax grain to the granary—I never thought that life would earn me fresh slander.

sentences.zh: 夫人情所不能止者，聖人弗禁，故君父至尊親，送其終也，有時而既。

translations.literal: What human feeling cannot stop, the sage does not forbid; therefore though lord and father are supremely venerated and intimate, in sending them off to the end there is a time when it is finished.
translations.idiomatic: What passion cannot restrain, even sages do not forbid: lord and father are the highest bonds, yet mourning for them also has its term.

sentences.zh: 臣之得罪，已三年矣。

translations.literal: Your servant's offense is already three years old.
translations.idiomatic: It is three years since my conviction.

sentences.zh: 田家作苦，歲時伏臘，亨羊□羔，鬥酒自勞。

translations.literal: The farmer's toil is bitter; at the seasonal offerings of summer and winter rest he boils sheep and [roasts] lamb, and with a flagon of wine refreshes himself.
translations.idiomatic: Farm work is brutal; on the summer and winter festival days I boil mutton, roast lamb, and toast myself with a jar of wine.

sentences.zh: 家本秦也，能為秦聲。

translations.literal: The family's origin was Qin; they could perform Qin songs.
translations.idiomatic: We are Qin folk by stock and still sing in the Qin mode.

sentences.zh: 婦，趙女也，雅善鼓瑟。

translations.literal: His wife was a Zhao woman, skilled at strumming the se.
translations.idiomatic: My wife is a Zhao girl who plucks the se better than I deserve.

sentences.zh: 奴婢歌者數人，酒後耳熱，仰天拊缶而呼烏烏。

translations.literal: Several slave-girl singers; after wine, ears hot, he faced heaven, beat the earthen jar, and cried 'wu wu.'
translations.idiomatic: A few bondmaids carry the tune; when the wine fires my blood I beat the clay fou and howl the old Qin refrains.

sentences.zh: 其詩曰：「田彼南山，蕪穢不治，種一頃豆，落而為其。

translations.literal: The poem says: 'I farm that southern hill; weeds choke it untended; I sowed a hundred qing of beans—they fell and turned to beanstalks.
translations.idiomatic: My impromptu lines run: 'South Mountain lies untilled, rank with weeds I never cleared; I sowed a full qing of beans—only beanstalks dropped where the pods should be.

sentences.zh: 人生行樂耳，須富貴何時！」

translations.literal: "Man's life is for pleasure—when will wealth and honour come!"
translations.idiomatic: "Life is for the living—wait for fortune and you wait forever!"

sentences.zh: 是日也，拂衣而喜，奮袖低卬，頓足起舞，誠淫荒無度，不知其不可也。

translations.literal: That day, brushing his robes in joy, waving sleeves high and low, stamping his feet and rising to dance—truly he was dissolute beyond measure and did not know it was impermissible.
translations.idiomatic: That day I shook out my sleeves, stamped the beat, and danced like a fool—shameless, and blind to how it looked.

sentences.zh: 惲幸有餘祿，方糴賤販貴，逐什一之利，此賈豎之事，污辱之處，惲親行之。

translations.literal: Yun fortunately had surplus salary; he was then buying cheap and selling dear, pursuing a one-in-ten profit—this is peddlers' work, a place of filth and insult, yet Yun personally did it.
translations.idiomatic: I still had a trickle of stipend, so I bought low and sold high for the merchant's ten-percent—work fit for a stall boy, yet I did it with my own hands.

sentences.zh: 下流之人，眾毀所歸，不寒而慄。

translations.literal: A man of the lower current is where the crowd's slander gathers; he shivers though it is not cold.
translations.idiomatic: Stand in the mud downstream and every slur finds you—you shiver without a wind.

sentences.zh: 雖雅知惲者，猶隨風而靡，尚何稱譽之有！

translations.literal: Even those who know Yun well still follow the wind and bend—what praise is there still to speak of!
translations.idiomatic: Even old friends bend with the rumor mill—what praise could reach me now?

sentences.zh: 董生不雲乎？

translations.literal: Did not Master Dong say?
translations.idiomatic: Has not Master Dong written?

sentences.zh: 「明明求仁義，常恐不能化民者，卿大夫意也；

translations.literal: 'Those who plainly seek benevolence and right, ever fearing they cannot transform the people—that is the intent of ministers and grandees;
translations.idiomatic: "Those who burn to spread benevolence and dread lest the people never mend—that is the temper of high ministers;

sentences.zh: 明明求財利，常恐困乏者，庶人之事也。」

translations.literal: those who plainly seek wealth and profit, ever fearing want—that is the business of commoners.'"
translations.idiomatic: those who plainly chase coin and fear poverty—that is the commoner's trade."

sentences.zh: 故「道不同，不相為謀。」
