translations.literal: Therefore 'when the Way differs, they do not counsel one another.'"
translations.idiomatic: Hence the saying: different roads, no shared counsel."

sentences.zh: 今子尚安得以卿大夫之制而責僕哉！

translations.literal: Now how can you, sir, still apply the standards of ministers and grandees to blame your servant!
translations.idiomatic: How dare you measure a farmer by a minister's yardstick?

sentences.zh: 夫西河魏土，文侯所興，有段干木、田子方之遺風，漂然皆有節概，知去就之分。

translations.literal: The land west of the river was Wei territory, where Marquis Wen flourished; it retains the surviving airs of Duan Ganmu and Tian Zifang—drifting, all have integrity and mettle, knowing the division between going and staying.
translations.idiomatic: Your own Xihe was the heart of Wei, raised by Marquess Wen and still breathing the austerity of Duan Ganmu and Tian Zifang—men who knew when to serve and when to walk away.

sentences.zh: 頃者，足下離舊土，臨安定，安定山谷之間，昆戎舊壤，子弟貪鄙，豈習俗之移人哉？

translations.literal: Recently you, sir, left your old soil and came to Anding; between the mountains and valleys of Anding lies the old ground of the Kun Rong—the sons and younger brothers are greedy and base—is it that custom moves men?
translations.idiomatic: You have moved to Anding, a gully land of Rong stock where the young men are coarse and grasping—has the border curdled your taste, or only revealed it?

sentences.zh: 於今乃睹子之志矣。

translations.literal: Only now do I see your intent.
translations.idiomatic: Now I see what you are really after.

sentences.zh: 方當盛漢之隆，願勉旃，毋多談。

translations.literal: Just as mighty Han is at its height, I hope you will strive—do not speak overmuch.
translations.idiomatic: Han still rides its zenith—spare me further sermons.

sentences.zh: 又惲兄子安平侯譚為典屬國，謂惲曰：「西河太守建平杜侯前以罪過出，今徵為御史大夫。

translations.literal: Moreover Yun's elder brother's son, Marquis of Anping Tan, was Commissioner for Dependent States; he said to Yun, "The Guardian of Xihe, Marquis Jianping Du, formerly went out on account of crime, but now has been summoned to be Imperial Counselor.
translations.idiomatic: His nephew Tan, marquis of Anping and commissioner for dependent states, added gossip: "Du, the Jianping marquis who was governor of Xihe, left office under a cloud but is back as imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 侯罪薄，又有功，且復用。」

translations.literal: The marquis's crime was light and he also had merit; he will be employed again."
translations.idiomatic: His guilt was slight and his service real—he will rise again."

sentences.zh: 惲曰：「有功何益？

translations.literal: Yun said, "What good is merit?
translations.idiomatic: Yun answered, "Merit buys nothing.

sentences.zh: 縣官不足為盡力。」

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven is not worth exhausting oneself for."
translations.idiomatic: This emperor is not worth breaking yourself for."

sentences.zh: 惲素與蓋寬饒、韓延壽善，譚即曰：「縣官實然，蓋司隸、韓馮翊皆盡力吏也，俱坐事誅。」

translations.literal: Yun had always been on good terms with Gai Kuanrao and Han Yanshou; Tan immediately said, "The Son of Heaven is indeed so—Metropolitan Superintendent Gai and Governor Han of Zuo Fengyi were both diligent officials, yet both sat on affairs and were executed."
translations.idiomatic: Yun had been close to Gai Kuanrao and Han Yanshou; Tan shot back, "If that is how the court treats men, Gai the metropolitan superintendent and Han the governor of Zuo Fengyi gave everything they had—and both died on the block."

sentences.zh: 會有日食變，騶馬猥佐成上書告惲「驕奢不悔過，日食之咎，此人所致。」

translations.literal: It happened that there was a solar-eclipse portent; the groom-attendant Cheng memorialized accusing Yun of 'arrogance and luxury without repenting; the blame for the eclipse lies with this man.'
translations.idiomatic: Then came an eclipse; a stable-hand named Cheng memorialized that Yun's swaggering refusal to reform had brought down heaven's rebuke.

sentences.zh: 章下廷尉案驗，得所予會宗書，宣帝見而惡之。

translations.literal: The memorial was sent down; the Commandant of Justice investigated and obtained the letter given to Huizong; Emperor Xuandi saw it and detested it.
translations.idiomatic: The throne forwarded the charge; the commandant seized the letter to Sun Huizong, and Xuandi read it with disgust.

sentences.zh: 廷尉當惲大逆無道，要斬。

translations.literal: The Commandant of Justice judged Yun guilty of great treason and impiety; he was cut in two at the waist.
translations.idiomatic: The commandant ruled capital treason; Yun was halved at the waist.

sentences.zh: 妻子徙酒泉郡。

translations.literal: Wife and children were banished to Jiuquan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: His wife and children were marched to Jiuquan.

sentences.zh: 譚坐不諫正惲，與相應，有怨望語，免為庶人。

translations.literal: Tan sat for failing to remonstrate with and correct Yun and for answering him with resentful words; he was dismissed to commoner status.
translations.idiomatic: Tan fell for not rebuking his uncle and for answering him with mutinous talk—stripped to commoner.

sentences.zh: 召拜成為郎，諸在位與惲厚善者，未央衛尉韋玄成、京兆尹張敞及孫會宗等，皆免官。

translations.literal: Cheng was summoned and appointed gentleman; all those in office who had been thick with Yun—Commandant of the Palace Guard Wei Xuancheng, Governor of the Capital Zhang Chang, and Sun Huizong among others—were all dismissed from office.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng was rewarded with a gentleman cadetship; every official who had stood close to Yun—Wei Xuancheng of the palace guard, Zhang Chang of the capital, Sun Huizong—lost his post.

sentences.zh: 蔡義，河內溫人也。

translations.literal: Cai Yi was a man of Wen in Henei.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yi came from Wen county in Henei.

sentences.zh: 以明經給事大將軍莫府。

translations.literal: By clarity in the classics he served in the Grand General's headquarters.
translations.idiomatic: His classical learning won him a clerkship in Huo Guang's headquarters.

sentences.zh: 家貧，常步行，資禮不逮眾門下，好事者相合為義買犢車，令乘之。

translations.literal: His family was poor; he always walked; his resources for ritual did not match the many retainers—men who loved a good deed clubbed together to buy Yi a calf-drawn cart and had him ride in it.
translations.idiomatic: He was too poor to keep pace on foot with richer clients, so well-wishers pooled cash and bought him a ox-drawn gig.

sentences.zh: 數歲，遷補覆盎城門候。

translations.literal: After several years he was transferred and appointed gatekeeper of Fugang Gate.
translations.idiomatic: Within a few years he was posted warden of Fugang Gate.

sentences.zh: 久之，詔求能為《韓詩》者，征義待詔，久不進見。

translations.literal: After a long while an edict sought one who could expound the Han version of the Odes; Yi was summoned as awaiting orders, but for long was not advanced to audience.
translations.idiomatic: An edict called for an expert on the Han Odes; Yi answered as a reserve scholar but languished without an audience.

sentences.zh: 義上疏曰：「臣山東草萊之人，行能亡所比，容貌不及眾，然而不棄人倫者，竊以聞道於先師，自托於經術也。

translations.literal: Yi submitted a memorial saying, "Your servant is a man of the grass and brush east of the mountains; in conduct and ability there is none to compare, in looks he does not match the crowd—yet he is not cast out from human relations because he has privately heard the Way from former teachers and entrusted himself to classical learning.
translations.idiomatic: He filed a memorial: "I am a weed scholar from east of the mountains—no beauty, no genius—yet I cling to human society because old masters taught me the classics."

sentences.zh: 願賜清閒之燕，得盡精思於前。」

translations.literal: I beg to be granted a clear and quiet banquet, that I may exhaust refined thought in your presence."
translations.idiomatic: Grant me one quiet audience and I will lay out every thought I have."

sentences.zh: 上召見義，說《詩》，甚說之，擢為光祿大夫給事中，進授昭帝。

translations.literal: The emperor summoned Yi for audience; he discoursed on the Odes; the emperor was very pleased, promoted him to Grand Palace Grandee and Palace Attendant, and advanced to instruct Emperor Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor received him, loved his lecture on the Odes, named him grand palace grandee with palace access, and set him to tutor young Emperor Zhao.

sentences.zh: 數歲，拜為少府，遷御史大夫，代楊敝為丞相，封陽平侯。

translations.literal: After several years he was appointed Junior Minister, promoted to Imperial Counselor, succeeded Yang Chang as chancellor, and was enfeoffed Marquis of Yangping.
translations.idiomatic: Within a few years he rose to junior minister, then imperial counselor, then replaced Yang Chang as chancellor with the marquisate of Yangping.

sentences.zh: 又以定策安宗廟益封，加賜黃金二百斤。

translations.literal: Again for fixing the succession and securing the ancestral temples his enfeoffment was increased, with an additional gift of two hundred catties of gold.
translations.idiomatic: He earned a larger fief and two hundred catties of gold for helping settle the imperial succession.

sentences.zh: 義為丞相時年八十餘，短小無鬚眉，貌似老嫗，行步俯僂，常兩吏扶夾乃能行。

translations.literal: When Yi was chancellor he was more than eighty years old, short in stature, without whiskers or eyebrows, his appearance like an old woman, his gait stooped; constantly two clerks had to support him on either side before he could walk.
translations.idiomatic: As chancellor he was past eighty, beardless, wizened as a crone, so stooped that two clerks had to prop him under the arms.

sentences.zh: 時大將軍光秉政，議者或言光置宰相不選賢，苟用可專制者。

translations.literal: At that time Grand General Huo Guang held power; some critics said Guang in setting up the chancellor did not choose the worthy but picked whoever could be dominated.
translations.idiomatic: Critics muttered that Huo Guang picked a chancellor he could puppeteer.

sentences.zh: 光聞之，謂侍中左右及官屬曰：「以為人主師當為宰相，何謂云云？

translations.literal: When Guang heard it he said to the palace attendants left and right and his office staff, "To be the ruler's teacher one ought to be chancellor—what is this talk?
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang heard and told his staff, "A tutor to the Son of Heaven should wear the chancellor's seal—what idle chatter is this?

sentences.zh: 此語不可使天下聞也。」

translations.literal: This language must not be allowed to spread through all under Heaven."
translations.idiomatic: Let that rumor go no further."

sentences.zh: 義為相四歲，薨，謚曰節侯。

translations.literal: Yi was chancellor four years, then died; his posthumous title was Marquis Jie.
translations.idiomatic: He died after four years in office and was canonized Jie, "the Prudent."

sentences.zh: 無子，國除。

translations.literal: He had no son; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: He left no heir; the marquisate lapsed.

sentences.zh: 陳萬年

translations.literal: Chen Wannian.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Wannian

sentences.zh: 陳萬年字幼公，沛郡相人也。

translations.literal: Chen Wannian, courtesy name Yougong, was a man of Xiang in Pei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Wannian, courtesy Yougong, was a native of Xiang in Pei.

sentences.zh: 為郡吏，察舉，至縣令，遷廣陵太守，以高弟入為右扶風，遷太僕。

translations.literal: He was a commandery clerk, recommended and promoted to county magistrate, transferred Guardian of Guangling, entered as Youfufeng with highest rank, and promoted Grand Coachman.
translations.idiomatic: He rose from county clerk through recommendation to magistrate, then governor of Guangling, then entered the capital as Youfufeng with top marks, then grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 萬年廉平，內行修，然善事人。

translations.literal: Wannian was incorrupt and fair, his inner conduct cultivated, yet he was skilled at serving men.
translations.idiomatic: He was honest and cultivated at home, yet a master flatterer in public.

sentences.zh: 賂遺外戚許、史，傾家自盡，尤事樂陵侯史高。

translations.literal: He bribed the maternal kin Xu and Shi, exhausting his household to the utmost, and especially served Marquis of Leling Shi Gao.
translations.idiomatic: He poured his fortune into gifts for the Xu and Shi consort clans and fawned especially on Marquis of Leling Shi Gao.

sentences.zh: 丞相丙吉病，中二千石上謁問疾。

translations.literal: Chancellor Bing Ji fell ill; those of full two thousand piculs and above called to inquire after his illness.
translations.idiomatic: When Chancellor Bing Ji fell ill, every minister at two thousand piculs paid a sick call.

sentences.zh: 遣家丞出謝，謝已皆去，萬年獨留，昏夜乃歸。

translations.literal: He sent out his household steward to thank them; after the thanks all departed, but Wannian alone remained and did not return until deep night.
translations.idiomatic: The steward bowed them out—Wannian alone lingered until midnight.

sentences.zh: 及吉病甚，上自臨，問以大臣行能。

translations.literal: When Ji was very gravely ill the emperor himself came to visit and asked about the conduct and ability of the great ministers.
translations.idiomatic: When Ji neared death the emperor came in person and asked which ministers were fit to succeed.

sentences.zh: 吉薦於定國、杜延年及萬年，萬年竟代定國為御史大夫八歲，病卒。

translations.literal: Ji recommended Yu Dinggu, Du Yannian, and Wannian; Wannian in the end replaced Dinggu as Imperial Counselor for eight years, then died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: Ji named Yu Dinggu, Du Yannian, and Wannian; Wannian eventually succeeded Yu Dinggu as imperial counselor for eight years until he died in office.

sentences.zh: 子咸字子康，年十八，以萬年任為郎。

translations.literal: His son Xian, courtesy name Zikang, at age eighteen through Wannian's appointment became a gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: His son Chen Xian, courtesy Zikang, entered as a gentleman cadet at eighteen through his father's rank.

sentences.zh: 有異材，抗直，數言事，刺譏近臣，書數十上，遷為左曹。

translations.literal: He had unusual talent, was upright and blunt, repeatedly spoke on affairs, satirizing the near ministers; his memorials numbered in the tens, and he was promoted to Left Assistant.
translations.idiomatic: He had rare ability, spoke bluntly, peppered the throne with memorials mocking the inner circle, and won promotion to left aide.

sentences.zh: 萬年嘗病，召咸教戒於床下，語至夜半，咸睡，頭觸屏風。

translations.literal: Wannian once fell ill and summoned Xian to instruct and warn him at the foot of the bed; he spoke until midnight; Xian slept, his head striking the screen.
translations.idiomatic: Once, ill in bed, Wannian lectured him past midnight until Xian nodded off and cracked his head on the screen.

sentences.zh: 萬年大怒，欲仗之，曰：「乃公教戒汝，汝反睡，不聽吾言，何也？」

translations.literal: Wannian was greatly angry and wished to beat him with a staff, saying, "Your old man instructs and warns you, yet you turn around and sleep, not listening to my words—why?"
translations.idiomatic: Wannian roared that he would cane him: "Your father is handing you the keys to survival and you snore?"

sentences.zh: 咸叩頭謝曰：「具曉所言，大要教咸諂也。」

translations.literal: Xian knocked his head in apology and said, "I fully understand what was said; the main gist was teaching Xian to flatter."
translations.idiomatic: Xian kowtowed: "I heard every word—the lesson was sycophancy."

sentences.zh: 萬年乃不復言。

translations.literal: Wannian then spoke no more.
translations.idiomatic: Wannian never finished the lecture.

sentences.zh: 萬年死後，元帝擢咸為御史中丞，總領州郡奏事，課第諸刺史，內執法殿中，公卿以下皆敬憚之。

translations.literal: After Wannian died Emperor Yuan promoted Xian to Palace Secretary to the Imperial Counselor, heading memorials from provinces and commanderies, ranking the governors, enforcing law within the palace—down to the dukes all feared and respected him.
translations.idiomatic: After his father's death Yuan appointed him palace secretary to the censorate, sorting provincial memorials, grading governors, and policing the inner court—every minister walked softly around him.

sentences.zh: 是時，中書令石顯用事顓權，咸頗言顯短，顯等恨之。

translations.literal: At that time Palace Secretary Shi Xian held power and monopolized authority; Chen Xian often spoke of Shi Xian's shortcomings; Shi Xian and his faction hated Chen Xian.
translations.idiomatic: Palace secretary Shi Xian monopolized power; Chen Xian exposed his faults and earned a bitter enemy.

sentences.zh: 時槐裡令硃雲殘酷殺不辜，有司舉奏，未下。

translations.literal: At that time the magistrate of Huaili, Zhu Yun, was cruel and killed the innocent; the authorities had raised a memorial, but it had not yet been approved.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun, magistrate of Huaili, had tortured innocents to death; a bill of impeachment sat unsigned on the desk.

sentences.zh: 咸素善雲，雲從刺候，教令上書自訟。

translations.literal: Xian had always been on good terms with Yun; Yun had him watched and instructed to memorialize in self-defence.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Xian, Zhu's friend, tipped him to file a counter-memorial.

sentences.zh: 於是石顯微伺知之，白奏咸漏洩省中語，下獄掠治，減死，髡為城旦，因廢。

translations.literal: Thereupon Shi Xian secretly spied and learned of it, reported in a memorial that Xian had leaked language from within the palace, cast him into prison and flogged him in investigation; his death sentence was commuted, he was shaved for wall corvée, and thereby ruined.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Xian's agents caught the leak, denounced Chen Xian for betraying palace secrets, had him flogged in jail, spared his life but shaved him for wall labor, and ended his career.

sentences.zh: 時，車騎將軍王音輔政，信用陳湯。

translations.literal: At that time General of Chariots and Cavalry Wang Yin assisted in government and trusted Chen Tang.
translations.idiomatic: Later General of Chariots and Cavalry Wang Yin governed and favored Chen Tang.

sentences.zh: 咸數賂遺湯，予書曰：「即蒙子公力，得入帝城，死不恨。」

translations.literal: Xian repeatedly bribed Tang and sent a letter saying, "If I rely on your, Zigong's, power to enter the imperial city, I will die without regret."
translations.idiomatic: Chen Xian showered Tang with gifts and wrote, "If you, Zigong, pull me back inside the walls, I can die content."

sentences.zh: 後竟征入為少府。

translations.literal: Later he was indeed summoned in as Junior Minister.
translations.idiomatic: Tang did bring him back as junior minister.

sentences.zh: 少府多寶物、屬官，咸皆鉤校，發其奸臧，沒入辜榷財物。

translations.literal: The Junior Minister's office had many treasures and subordinate offices; Xian audited them all, exposed their crafty corruption, and confiscated goods seized under the monopoly statutes.
translations.idiomatic: He inventoried the ministry's vaults and bureaus, exposed every embezzlement, and impounded the hoarded monopoly profits.

sentences.zh: 官屬及諸中宮黃門、鉤盾、掖庭官吏，舉奏按論，畏咸，皆失氣。

translations.literal: Subordinate offices and all the inner-palace yellow-gate attendants, hook-shield and rear-palace clerks—he raised memorials to investigate and sentence them; they feared Xian and all lost their breath.
translations.idiomatic: Yellow gates, hook-shield guards, harem clerks—he impeached them in batches until they went pale at his name.

sentences.zh: 為少府三歲，與翟方進有隙。

translations.literal: As Junior Minister for three years he had a rift with Zhai Fangjin.
translations.idiomatic: After three years he quarreled with Zhai Fangjin.

sentences.zh: 方進為丞相，奏：「咸前為郡守，所在殘酷，毒螫加於吏民。

translations.literal: Fangjin as chancellor memorialized: "Xian formerly as guardian was cruel where he ruled, venom stinging officials and people.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai, now chancellor, charged that Chen Xian had been a savage governor who poisoned every place he ruled.

sentences.zh: 主守盜，受所監。

translations.literal: He guarded and stole, accepting bribes from those he supervised.
translations.idiomatic: He stole from the treasuries he guarded and took bribes from his own staff.

sentences.zh: 而官媚邪臣陳湯以求薦舉。

translations.literal: Yet as an official he fawned on the evil minister Chen Tang to seek recommendation.
translations.idiomatic: He debased himself flattering Chen Tang for a recommendation.

sentences.zh: 苟得無恥，不宜處位。」

translations.literal: Shamelessly grasping without scruple, he is not fit to hold office."
translations.idiomatic: He is shameless and unfit for office."

sentences.zh: 咸坐免。

translations.literal: Xian sat guilty and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Xian was cashiered.

sentences.zh: 頃之，紅陽侯立舉咸方正，為光祿大夫給事中，方進復奏免之。

translations.literal: Shortly thereafter Marquis of Hongyang Wang Li recommended Xian as upright and square, and he became Grand Palace Grandee and Palace Attendant; Fangjin again memorialized and dismissed him.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Li of Hongyang soon nominated him as "upright"; he won back a palace post until Zhai struck him down again.

sentences.zh: 後數年，立有罪就國，方進奏歸咸故郡，以憂死。

translations.literal: Several years later Li committed a crime and went to his fief; Fangjin memorialized to return Xian to his old commandery, where he died of grief.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Li later fell, Zhai had Chen Xian packed off to his home commandery, where he died of shame.

sentences.zh: 鄭弘字稚卿，泰山剛人也。

translations.literal: Zheng Hong, courtesy name Zhiqing, was a man of Gang in Taishan.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Hong, courtesy Zhiqing, came from Gang in Taishan.

sentences.zh: 兄昌字次卿，亦好學，皆明經，通法律政事。

translations.literal: His elder brother Chang, courtesy name Ciqing, also loved learning; both were clear in the classics and versed in statutes, law, and government affairs.
translations.idiomatic: His elder brother Zheng Chang, courtesy Ciqing, loved books as well; both mastered the classics and the code.

sentences.zh: 次卿為太原、涿郡太守，弘為南陽太守，皆著治跡，條教法度，為後所述。

translations.literal: Ciqing was Guardian of Taiyuan and Zhuo commanderies, Hong was Guardian of Nanyang; both showed traces of good order, regulations and models, and were later recounted.
translations.idiomatic: Ciqing governed Taiyuan and Zhuo, Hong governed Nanyang—each left a clean record and statutes others copied.

sentences.zh: 次卿用刑罰深，不如弘平，遷淮陽相，以高第入為右扶風，京師稱之。

translations.literal: Ciqing used punishments deeply, not as even as Hong; he was transferred Chancellor of Huaiyang, entered with highest rank as Youfufeng, and was praised in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Ciqing ruled with a heavy hand; Hong was the fairer judge. Ciqing moved to chancellor of Huaiyang, then entered as Youfufeng with top marks and won the capital's praise.

sentences.zh: 代韋玄成為御史大夫。

translations.literal: He succeeded Wei Xuancheng as Imperial Counselor.
translations.idiomatic: He replaced Wei Xuancheng as imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 六歲，坐與京房論議免，語在《房傳》。

translations.literal: Within six years he sat for discussing and debating with Jing Fang of the capital and was dismissed; the account is in Fang's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Six years later he lost his post for debating policy with Jing Fang of the capital; the story is told in Jing Fang's biography.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：所謂鹽鐵議者，起始元中，徵文學賢良問以治亂，皆對願罷郡國鹽鐵、酒榷均輸，務本抑末，毋與天下爭利，然後教化可興。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: What is called the salt-and-iron debate began in the Shiyuan era, when men of letters and worthy candidates were summoned and asked about order and disorder; all replied that they wished to abolish commandery and kingdom monopolies on salt and iron, wine excise, and equal-delivery transport, to devote effort to the root and check the branch, not to contend for profit with all under Heaven—only then could transformation be raised.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's verdict: the salt-and-iron debate opened in the Shiyuan years, when the court summoned literati and worthies and asked how to cure the realm's ills. They answered in one voice—scrap the state monopolies on salt, iron, wine, and the equal-supply caravans, strengthen agriculture and starve the merchant, stop competing with the people for coin—only then could moral instruction take root.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫弘羊以為此乃所以安邊竟，制四夷，國家大業，不可廢也。

translations.literal: Imperial Counselor Hongyang thought that this was the means whereby to secure the border marches, control the four barbarians, and accomplish the state's great enterprise—it could not be abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Counselor Sang Hongyang countered that those policies guarded the frontier, cowed the four quarters, and underpinned the dynasty—they could not simply be junked.

sentences.zh: 當時相詰難，頗有其議文。

translations.literal: At the time they cross-examined one another; there are rather full texts of their arguments.
translations.idiomatic: The two sides cross-examined each other at length, and much of that dialogue survives.

sentences.zh: 至宣帝時，汝南桓寬次公治《公羊春秋》舉為郎，至廬江太守丞，博通善屬文，推衍鹽鐵之議，增廣條目，極其論難，著數萬言，亦欲以究治亂，成一家之法焉。

translations.literal: By Emperor Xuandi's time Huan Kuan of Runan, courtesy name Cigong, who studied the Gongyang commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals and had been raised to gentleman, reached assistant to the Guardian of Lujiang; broadly learned and skilled at composition, he expanded the salt-and-iron debate, widened the entries, carried argument and rebuttal to the limit, and wrote several tens of thousands of characters, also wishing thereby to investigate order and disorder and complete a school of method of his own.
translations.idiomatic: Under Xuandi, Huan Kuan of Runan—who had mastered the Gongyang Annals, risen to gentleman, and risen to aide to the governor of Lujiang—was erudite and eloquent. He reworked the debate into a book, added topics, pushed every argument to its limit, and wrote tens of thousands of characters, hoping to settle what brings order and what brings chaos in a treatise that stands on its own.

sentences.zh: 其辭曰：「觀公卿賢良文學之議，『異乎吾所聞』。

translations.literal: Its wording says: 'Observing the debate of the dukes, ministers, worthy candidates, and men of letters—"it differs from what I have heard."'
translations.idiomatic: His preface reads: "Reading the speeches of ministers and worthies, I find them far from what I had been taught."

sentences.zh: 聞汝南硃生言，當此之時，英俊並進，賢良茂陵唐生、文學魯國萬生之徒六十有餘人咸聚闕庭，舒六藝之風，陳治平之原，知者贊其慮，仁者明其施，勇者見其斷，辯者騁其辭，斷斷焉，行行焉，雖未詳備，斯可略觀矣。

translations.literal: Hearing what Master Zhu of Runan said—at that time the talented advanced together; more than sixty such as the worthy Tang Sheng of Maoling, the man of letters Wan Sheng of Lu, and their like all gathered in the palace court, spreading the airs of the six arts, setting forth the sources of peace and order; the wise praised their plans, the benevolent clarified their measures, the brave showed their decisiveness, the eloquent gave rein to their words—decidedly, resolutely, though not exhaustively detailed, thereby one may broadly observe.
translations.idiomatic: Runan's Master Zhu told me how, in that hall, talent pressed forward together: more than sixty worthies—Tang Sheng of Maoling, Wan Sheng of Lu, and the rest—thronged the court, unfurled the six classics, traced the springs of good government. Wisdom applauded their designs, humanity their policies, courage their resolve, rhetoric their language. Sharp, steadfast, if not complete—it is still a panorama worth studying.

sentences.zh: 中山劉子推言王道，撟當世，反諸正，彬彬然弘博君子也。

translations.literal: Liu Zitui of Zhongshan discoursed on the kingly Way, straightened what was awry in the age, returned matters to the correct norm—truly a cultivated, broad-minded gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Zitui of Zhongshan preached the royal Way, bent the age back toward rectitude, and carried himself like a true junzi—learned, ample, composed.

sentences.zh: 九江祝生奮史魚之節，發憤懣，譏公卿，介然直而不撓，可謂不畏強圉矣。

translations.literal: Zhu Sheng of Jiujiang roused the integrity of Ziyu of Wei, vented indignant resentment, satirized the dukes and ministers, stood firm and straight without bending—he may be called one who did not fear the mighty.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Sheng of Jiujiang took up the unbending spirit of Ziyu of Wei, poured out honest rage, mocked the high ministers, and would not bow—here was a man who feared no power.

sentences.zh: 桑大夫據當世，合時變，上權利之略，雖非正當，巨儒宿學不能自解，博物通達之士也。

translations.literal: Minister Sang grasped the present age, fitted the changes of the times, offered stratagems of power and profit; though not orthodox, great scholars and long-studied men could not untangle themselves—he was a man broadly learned and penetrating.
translations.idiomatic: Minister Sang spoke to his own day, adapted to shifting circumstance, and spun schemes of profit and control. His doctrine was not classical, yet the greatest academicians could not refute him—whatever else, he was formidably learned.

sentences.zh: 然攝公卿之柄，不師古始，放於末利，處非其位，行非其道，果隕其性，以及厥宗。

translations.literal: Yet he seized the handle of the dukes and ministers, did not model himself on antiquity at the beginning, gave rein to branch profits, occupied a position not his, pursued a Way not his—in the end he ruined himself and reached to his clan.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he seized the levers of state, scorned the models of antiquity, chased the merchant's margin, sat in a chair that did not fit him, and walked a path that was not his—until he destroyed himself and dragged his kin down with him.

sentences.zh: 車丞相履伊、呂之列，當軸處中，括囊不言，容身而去，彼哉！

translations.literal: The Cart Chancellor trod the ranks of Yi Yin and Lü Wang, sat on the axle at the center, tied the sack shut and did not speak, preserved his person and departed—such men!
translations.idiomatic: Che Qianqiu stood where Yi Yin and Lü Wang once stood, yet at the hub of power he sealed his lips like a tied purse, never counseled aloud, saved his skin, and withdrew—such men!

sentences.zh: 彼哉！

translations.literal: Such men!
translations.idiomatic: Such men!

sentences.zh: 若夫丞相、御史兩府之士，不能正議以輔宰相，成同類，長同行，阿意苟合，以說其上，『斗筲之徒，何足選也！』」

translations.literal: As for the gentlemen of the two bureaus of chancellor and Imperial Counselor, who could not set right debate to assist the chancellor, who formed cliques of the same kind, lengthened lines of the same walk, bent their intent to easy agreement to please those above—'fellows of the pint and peck basket—how could they be worth selecting!'"
translations.idiomatic: And the clerks of chancellor and censor yamens who dared not speak straight to aid their chief, who flocked with their own kind, lengthened each other's faults, and flattered upward for favor—"those pint-measure politicians were never worth the choosing!"
